EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense storm system moved across the Upper Midwest on December 15th and produced a rare severe weather outbreak across southeast Minnesota. One person was killed near Rochester (Olmsted County) when a large tree fell on the victim. Twelve tornadoes occurred across parts of Mower, Fillmore, Houston, Dodge, Winona and Wabasha Counties. Eight of these tornadoes were rated EF1 with the others rated EF0.  The tornadoes largely moved through rural areas damaging trees, power lines and farm buildings with the exception of an EF0 tornado that moved through the eastern sections of Preston (Fillmore County) and and EF1 tornado that tracked across Homer (Winona County). In addition to the tornadoes, damaging winds occurred with several reports of wind speeds over 70 mph. The highest recorded wind gusts were 85 mph in Plainview (Wabasha County), 84 mph in Harmony (Fillmore County) and Mabel (Fillmore County) and 83 mph south of Grand Meadow (Mower County). A state of emergency was declared in Fillmore County. The Governor of Minnesota authorized emergency assistance for the following counties: Dodge, Fillmore, Houston, Mower, Olmsted and Wabasha.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This short-lived EF0 tornado moved over rural areas southeast of Lewiston. Farm outbuildings, a garage and trees were damaged by the tornado.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF1 tornado formed on a ridge top southwest of Homer, moved northeast across Homer before moving out over the Mississippi River and into Trempealeau County, Wisconsin. The main damage occurred on the ridge top to trees and outbuildings. By the time the tornado reached Homer, it was weakening and damage was mainly limited to trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a moviegoer at an east metro drive-in theater as storms moved through the area. The man was hit about 10:25 p.m at the Vali-Hi Drive-In on just north of Interstate 94 in Lake Elmo.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a moviegoer at an east metro drive-in theater as storms moved through the area. The man was struck about 10:25 p.m CDT, Thursday, August, 5th at the Vali-Hi Drive-In just north of Interstate 94 in Lake Elmo.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe storms which developed across western South Dakota during the evening of June 10 translated eastward through the overnight hours. An increasing low-level jet enhanced shear, with an environment strongly supportive of developing outflow pools with DCAPE of 1500-2000 J/kg. Storms congealed into a linear bowing structure through the late overnight and early morning hours, producing strong to damaging wind gusts as storms moved into southwest Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A road weather mesonet station (MN007) near Interstate 90 mile marker 35 northeast of Rushmore measured a thunderstorm wind gust of 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large outflow boundary from a late night convective complex across northeast South Dakota and west central MN pushed southward, and interacted with a strong low-level jet. With steep mid-level lapse rates present, convective initiation resulted in intense updrafts along the boundary which produced large hail.

Within a cluster of warm advection elevated thunderstorms, 1000-1500 J/kg of elevated instability and 40 knots of deep-layer shear allowed storms to produce marginally large hail during the afternoon.
Severe thunderstorms impacted portions of west-central Minnesota on October 13th, producing funnel clouds and damaging thunderstorm winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A persistent funnel cloud was noted, with photos and videos posted to social media. Eyewitnesses reported that there were no touchdowns or damage observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorm activity produced damaging wind gusts and tornadoes on October 9th across portions of west-central Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Possible tornado reported by the Hubbard County Sheriffs Office. Damage was reported on Industrial Park Road, near the intersection of HWY 71. Damage included metal roofing, uprooted trees, and road signs blown down. Peak winds estimated at 100 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorm activity produced damaging wind gusts and tornadoes on October 9th across portions of west-central Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of several trees down and roads impassable due to branch debris in town. Time estimated based on radar.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An RWIS station in Herman measured a wind gust to 86 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms impacted portions of west-central Minnesota on October 13th, producing funnel clouds and damaging thunderstorm winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS Storm Damage Survey of the northwest shore of Eagle Lake, 3 miles west of Frazee, revealed two uprooted 18 to 24-inch pine trees and a flipped dock. Another dock had three aluminum deck panels ripped from it. Eyewitnesses to the event stated trees fell and aluminum deck panels flew in the same direction, indicating straight-line microburst winds. One tree caused a slight indentation of an asphalt driveway. Evaluation of damage indicators resulted in estimated 75 to 90 mph winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated supercell developed across south central South Dakota late evening along an advancing cold frontal boundary. With appreciable DCAPE and deep-layer effective shear, upscale growth occurred rapidly with the development of widespread damaging winds, which spread out of eastern South Dakota into Southwest Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm wind gusts produced crop damage, downed several large branches and uprooted a large tree. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a cold front pushed into an environment of large MLCAPE (2000-3000 J/kg) and high DCAPE (1000 J/kg), scattered elevated thunderstorms developed on the nose of a 40 knot low-level jet across southwest Minnesota. These storms dropped south southeast through northwest Iowa during the evening producing periodic large hail and some damaging wind gusts as the low-level jet veered ahead of the advancing boundary.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong fall storm system affected much of the Northland on November 11 into the 12th. Warm temperatures ahead of an area of low pressure led to a rain event across the southern part of the area while colder air moving in from the north led to a changeover to snow from the International Falls area southward into the Iron Range area earlier than forecast. This lead to snowfall of 6 to 8 inches from southern Koochiching County into central St. Louis County where a colder pocket of air moved in and kept snow going earlier and longer than anticipated. The snow along the Iron Range led to snow and ice covered roads and multiple wrecks. One wreck near Gilbert resulted in two fatalities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong storm brought heavy snow to much of the county in a span of just over 12 hours. Snowfall totals of 6 to 8 inches were reported with the highest report being 8 inches near Big Falls.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions persisted across much of northeastern Minnesota during the month of November, but improvement was seen as the month progressed. Precipitation varied across the region with the Brainerd Lakes area eastward to the I-35 corridor receiving slightly below normal precipitation for the month while areas to the north received above normal precipitation, especially along the North Shore into eastern Cook County. Much of the region began the month with a mix of D2 Severe Drought and D3 Extreme Drought with areas of D1 Moderate Drought and D0 Abnormally Dry over far southern areas. Rainfall during the first two weeks of the month led to the removal of the areas of D3 Extreme Drought across Koochiching and St. Louis counties with losses of D2 Severe Drought across the region as well. The pattern turned less active overall for the latter half of the month, but above normal rainfall along the North Shore led to further reduction in the area of D3 Extreme Drought with the update on the 30th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Most of the area was under D3 Extreme Drought conditions to begin the month outside of the eastern tip of Cook County which was in D2 Severe Drought. Slightly above normal precipitation thorugh the first few weeks of the month resulted in the reduction of D3 Extreme Drought across the western third of Lake County, being replaced with D2 Severe Drought with the update on the 16th. Additional precipitation over the remainder of the month on the order of 1 to 2 inches plus pushed the monthly total well above normal for most of the area except for far northern Lake County. Further reduction in the area of D3 Extreme Drought across the southern reaches of both Lake and Cook counties was seen with the update on the 30th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The afternoon of Monday, April 5th, a frontal boundary was lying across southwest Minnesota, then northeast toward northwest Wisconsin. Ahead of this front, temperatures rose into the low to mid-80s with modest MLCAPEs of 1000 J/Kg. Mid-level lapse rates were quite impressive and over 8 C/Km. Thus, the potential of large hail was evident as effective wind shear values were 40-50 knots. By the afternoon, several thunderstorms developed west of the Twin Cities and moved northeast between St. Cloud and the Twin Cities. Numerous reports of large hail fell in this area with the large hailstone measured at 1.75. By late evening, most of the severe weather had ended.

A trough of low pressure moving through the Minnesota Arrowhead, a tight pressure gradient and downsloping winds along the terrain ridge of the North Shore of Lake Superior led to some very gusty winds for a period the morning of the 19th. The Grand Marais Harbor gusted to 58 mph while a personal weather station located in a creek valley on the terrain slope recorded a gust to 72 mph. Despite the strong winds, no damage reports were received.

Scattered thunderstorms developed across southwest Minnesota and moved rapidly northeast during the morning of Tuesday, April 27th. These thunderstorms produced 1.25 diameter hail north of Trimont, Minnesota before weakening. There was also a report of severe hail northeast of Trimont, but most of the hailstones were less than 3/4 of an inch.

The last two nights of the arctic outbreak were the most widespread in terms extreme wind chills. On the night of the 13th, a surface low over Lake Superior coupled with high pressure over the Northern Plains set up a gradient over the Northland, keeping winds in place through the night and into the morning of the 14th. The entirety of the Northland saw extreme wind chills and air temperatures in the 30s and 40s below zero. This was the eighth night of an arctic outbreak across the region that lasted over a week.

The last night of the arctic outbreak was once again widespread, affecting almost all of the Northland. High pressure was in place over the region leading to optimized radiational cooling conditions and the slightest breeze of wind was enough to send wind chills into dangerous criteria. Air temperatures dropped into the 30s and 40s below zero once again with wind chills as low as -51F. This was the ninth and final night of an arctic outbreak across the region that lasted over a week.

Another shot of arctic air settled in over southeast Minnesota and produced dangerously cold wind chills of 35 to 45 below zero from the late evening of February 13th into the morning of the 15th. The wind chills occurred with air temperatures in the single digits and teens below zero along with winds of 10 to 15 mph. The coldest recorded wind chills were 44 below zero on the morning of the 14th at mesonet stations near Dexter (Mower County) and Kasson (Dodge County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dangerously cold wind chills of 35 to 45 below zero occurred from the late evening of February 13th into the morning of the 15th. These wind chills occurred with temperatures in the single digits and teens below zero and winds of 10 to 15 mph. The coldest recorded wind chill was 42 below zero at a mesonet station near Stewartville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong cold front was accompanied by a narrow band of moderate to heavy snowfall, as well as wind gusting to 30 to 45 mph. Most areas saw a very brief period of rain, freezing rain or sleet before changing to snow, which was accompanied by a temperature fall of 10 to 20 degrees over a six hour period. Contraction of the band occurred due to strong elevated instability, with isolated thunder occurring in southeast South Dakota and southwest Minnesota. While most areas received around an inch or two of snowfall, the band peaked in intensity around the Sioux Falls area with up to three to five inch accumulation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall was briefly heavy and totaled 1.8 inches near Lake Benton. Roads became hazardous, and frequent wind gusts as high as 35 mph near Lake Benton resulted in several hours of blowing snow with visibility as low as one-half mile.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The coldest arctic air of the season settled across northwest and west-central MN. Minimum morning temperatures ranged from minus 25F to minus 48F with wind chills ranging from minus 45F to minus 60F.  Daytime high temperatures remained well below zero in most locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cooperative Observer, AWOS, and Mesonet sensors reported morning low wind chill temperatures of from minus 40F to minus 50F, each day. Air temperatures of minus 40F or colder were recorded.

A massive arctic plunge into the Northern Plains brought almost a week of dangerous and life threatening wind chills to the region. The worst conditions were recorded between February 13 and February 16 when wind chills occasionally dropped as low as 35 below to 55 below zero. Numerous record low maximum and minimum temperatures were also established. The areal extent of the extreme cold in the central portions of the U.S. led to stress on the power grid, which necessitated several short-term rolling power outages impacting more than 10,000 customers during the peak of the cold.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: During several periods over three days with wind chill below -35, values reached a minimum of -50 near Lake Benton, -47 5 miles south of Hendricks and -46 at Ivanhoe.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Bitterly cold air with temperatures from 10 to 20 below zero settled in over southeast Minnesota during the early morning hours of February 7th. Combined with northwest winds around 10 mph, dangerously cold winds chills of 35 to 45 below zero occurred. The coldest recorded wind chill was -44 by the observing equipment at the Rochester (Olmsted County) airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The combination of air temperatures in the teens below zero and northwest winds around 10 mph produced dangerously cold wind chills between 35 and 45 below zero. The coldest recorded wind chill was 42 below zero by the observing equipment at the Preston airport and a mesonet station east of Canton.

Combined with northwest winds around 10 mph, dangerously cold winds chills of 35 to 45 below zero occurred. The coldest recorded wind chill was -44 by the observing equipment at the Rochester (Olmsted County) airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The combination of air temperatures in the teens below zero and northwest winds around 10 mph produced dangerously cold wind chills between 35 and 45 below zero. The coldest recorded wind chill was 43 below zero by a mesonet station near Dexter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic air mass began to move into the Northland the night of the 5th and really settled in the night of the 6th. Air temperatures in the 20s to 30s below zero Fahrenheit were observed with light winds that led to wind chills as low as -50F. This was the first night of an extended cold outbreak across the region that lasted over a week.

Repeated shots of bitterly cold arctic air penetrated through northwest MN. Minimum morning temperatures ranged from minus 15F to minus 30F with wind chills ranging from minus 40F to minus 55F.  Daytime high temperatures remained well below zero in most locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cooperative Observer, AWOS, and Mesonet sensors reported morning low wind chill temperatures of from minus 35F to minus 48F, each day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a cold front pushed into an environment of large MLCAPE (2000-3000 J/kg) and high DCAPE (1000 J/kg), scattered elevated thunderstorms developed on the nose of a 40 knot low-level jet across southwest Minnesota. These storms dropped south southeast through northwest Iowa during the evening producing periodic large hail and some damaging wind gusts as the low-level jet veered ahead of the advancing boundary.

A potent and persistent outbreak of Arctic air affected the area from February 6th through the 17th. The coldest days of the outbreak for many occurred Valentine's Day weekend when high temperatures averaged in the single digits below zero. On February 14th, low temperatures dropped into the 20s to the 30s degrees below zero range. Extreme wind chills of 35 degrees to 45 degrees below zero also occurred for several days during the outbreak. | |The magnitude of the cold during this outbreak was fairly rare compared to the past 50 years, at least in terms of the persistence of the Arctic air. This was especially impressive considering the lack of deep, fresh snow cover across of the area. If there had been widespread deep, fresh snowpack ahead of this Arctic outbreak, low temperatures would have been more severe and more often approaching record territory. Wheaton saw five consecutive days with highs below zero, which is among the top ten most extended stretches since 1914. Wheaton also experienced five straight days with lows falling to 20 below zero or colder, ranking in the top five stretches.||Impacts from this extreme and persistent cold include some frozen and/or broken water pipes (the limited snow depth did not help in this regard) and froze-over home sewer vents, dead vehicle batteries, school delays, and church cancellations. The prolonged cold caused significant strains to the power grid as demand spiked both locally and across several other states. Many customers were at least briefly without power locally, particularly during the morning of Tue, Feb 16th. Concerns for rolling blackouts lingered for several days in this regard due to the continued extreme demand/strain, and people were asked to conserve energy however possible.

A massive arctic plunge into the Northern Plains brought almost a week of dangerous and life threatening wind chills to the region. The worst conditions were recorded between February 13 and February 16 when wind chills occasionally dropped as low as 35 below to 55 below zero. Numerous record low maximum and minimum temperatures were also established. The areal extent of the extreme cold in the central portions of the U.S. led to stress on the power grid, which necessitated several short-term rolling power outages impacting more than 10,000 customers during the peak of the cold.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: During several periods over three days with wind chill below -35, values reached a minimum of -45 at Marshall and -41 at Tracy and Minneota.

An upper level low parked over Hudson Bay continued to pull arctic air down across the Northland. Air temperatures fell into the -20 to -36F range with light winds leading to bitter wind chills as low as -45F. This was the fourth night of an extended arctic outbreak that lasted over a week. While very cold temperatures were observed the previous night, winds were too light to generate dangerously cold wind chills.

Arctic air was still in place over the region the night of the 11th, but a combination of clouds and lighter winds tempered the overall drop in temperatures. The northwestern part of Cass County was able to clear out and see temperatures drop to around -30F. Coupled with light winds, a few hours of wind chills below -40F were observed in the hours around sunrise on the 12th. This was the sixth night of an extended arctic outbreak across the region that lasted over a week. The airmass moderated slightly the previous night of the 10th with readings only falling into the 20s Fahrenheit below zero. Light winds resulted in wind chills only in the 30s below zero, falling short of criteria.

The last night of the arctic outbreak was once again widespread, affecting almost all of the Northland. High pressure was in place over the region leading to optimized radiational cooling conditions and the slightest breeze of wind was enough to send wind chills into dangerous criteria. Air temperatures dropped into the 30s and 40s below zero once again with wind chills as low as -51F. This was the ninth and final night of an arctic outbreak across the region that lasted over a week.

A band of snowfall developed within a confluent trough across the region. Strong mid-level frontogenesis and slight instability above the frontal zone produced banding, which was transient and limited snowfall amounts to generally 2 to 6 inches. Outside the heavier snowfall, some light mixed-type precipitation occurred, but had limited impacts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall was heavy at times between 0100-0800CST, and produced treacherous travel conditions. Snowfall totaled 8.0 inches at Minneota, 5.0 inches at Tracy and 4.0 inches at Marshall.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 3.0 inches at Pipestone and resulted in dangerous travel conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Repeated shots of bitterly cold arctic air penetrated through northwest MN. Minimum morning temperatures ranged from minus 15F to minus 30F with wind chills ranging from minus 40F to minus 55F.  Daytime high temperatures remained well below zero in most locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cooperative Observer, AWOS, and Mesonet sensors reported morning low wind chill temperatures of from minus 35F to minus 46F, each day.

Isolated thunderstorms developed along a nearly stationary boundary south of Interstate 90 in the very early hours of April 27.  An isolated storm made use of 30 to 40 knots of effective speed shear and elevated instability of 500-1000 J/kg to organize long enough to produce severe hail.

An early season Winter Storm moved into the Northern Plains on the afternoon and evening of the 13th, producing widespread and high sustained winds with areas of light snow across portions of southeast ND and west-central MN, with areas of much heavier snow across portions of northwest MN.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds sustained at 40 mph or greater, with gusts to 53 mph, were measured by MNDOT RWIS stations in southern Wilkin and western Grant Counties. This occurred along with very light snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early Spring storm caused a few severe storms to develop across portions of southern Minnesota during the afternoon of Wednesday, March 10th. ||A cold front moved southward across southern Minnesota before stalling from south-central Minnesota, northeast to the southeast part of the Twin Cities metro area by Wednesday morning. Behind the front, temperatures fell and held in the 30s and 40s all day. Along and ahead of this front, temperatures rose into the 50s and 60s with sunshine developing in the afternoon. Wind shear values were quite high as a 50-80 knot 500 hPa jet moved overhead. At the surface, a strong low-pressure system moved from north-central Iowa, north-northeastward along this frontal boundary. Scattered thunderstorms developed in the afternoon in the warm sector of the storm system. Some rotation was noted as the storms propagated northeast. However, most of the reports were rotating wall clouds vs. funnel clouds or tornadoes. There was a report of large hail near Waterville as this storm moved across the area. The strongest storm moved across Dakota County and produced several reports of large hail; the largest was 2.5 inches in diameter which fell near Inver Grove Heights. Almost all of the reports came from social media pictures and relayed to the NWS. ||By the early evening, most of the storms weakened and moved off into west-central Wisconsin.

Well behind this front, temperatures fell and held in the 30s and 40s all day. ||During the morning and early afternoon, light precipitation developed across west-central and central Minnesota. The heaviest, with isolated reports higher then 8 inches, was across northern Todd County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several observations across Stevens County, especially over the northern portions, received between 5 to 7 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening of Sunday, March 14th, a very large storm system began to move out into the plains with a broad circulation across most of the Northern Plains/Upper Midwest. The surface low was centered across Kansas with several weaker shortwaves moving around the broad circulation through Monday night, March 15th. Initially, very dry air below 15,000 feet was noted from Sunday evening and Monday morning soundings at the Chanhassen NWS. This kept most of the precipitation from reaching the ground until the atmosphere started to saturate from the continuous feed of mid-level moisture.||Surface observations at Fairmont, Minnesota reported light precipitation around 1 AM CST. This area of precipitation slowly spread northward during the morning, reaching Redwood Falls, New Ulm, Mankato, and Waseca around 330 AM CST. The precipitation didn't reach the southwest metro area until 9-10 AM CST as the dry easterly flow continued. ||As the system began to pivot and the deeper moisture moved northward, the precipitation ended in portions of southwest Minnesota. However, one continuous feed of mid-level moisture across southeast Minnesota, and into south-central Minnesota kept a steady area of moderate to heavy snowfall. This area of heavier precipitation was able to move as far north as the southern Twin Cities metro area during the mid-late afternoon. However, most of the forcing with this system was weakening and the precipitation started to end. By the early evening, the steadier snowfall had ended with some lingering light snow across east-central and central Minnesota. ||The heavier snowfall occurred in a 25-50 mile band from New Ulm, southeast and east toward Mankato, Waseca, and Owatonna. The heaviest was reported around Mankato where there were a couple of reports of 10 to 11 inches in a 12 hour period. Another 10 to 11 inch report was near Nicollet, Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several sources of weather observations indicated 4 to 8 inches of snow fell across western portions of Goodhue County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large slow-moving area of low pressure wandered from Colorado to eastern South Dakota over a two-day period. Precipitation circulated back into northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota, which started as a period of rainfall prior to evening on March 14. Rain mixed and changed to snow during the late evening and early overnight. Snow became heavy at times during overnight, with snowfall rates up to 2 inches per hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall was heavy at times between 0000CST and 0400CST on March 15, resulting in very hazardous travel conditions. Totals of 6.0 inches at Edgerton and 5.5 inches were recorded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The afternoon of Monday, April 5th, a frontal boundary was lying across southwest Minnesota, then northeast toward northwest Wisconsin. Ahead of this front, temperatures rose into the low to mid-80s with modest MLCAPEs of 1000 J/Kg. Mid-level lapse rates were quite impressive and over 8 C/Km. Thus, the potential of large hail was evident as effective wind shear values were 40-50 knots. By the afternoon, several thunderstorms developed west of the Twin Cities and moved northeast between St. Cloud and the Twin Cities. Numerous reports of large hail fell in this area with the large hailstone measured at 1.75. By late evening, most of the severe weather had ended.

Abnormally warm temperatures were present across the region leading up to this event with highs in the mid 50s. The warm air near the surface set the stage for a wintry mix followed by heavy wet snow by the evening hours. Additionally, a surface low was deepening as it encroached on the Northland, bringing gusty winds with the Duluth airport reporting a gust of 55 mph just before midnight. The combination of wet, heavy snow clumping to trees and the enhanced winds lead to several downed trees and power outages throughout the area. Snowfall totals were highest across the Arrowhead, ranging from 6 to 11 inches.

Thunderstorms that developed earlier across southwest Minnesota, decayed and moved eastward across south-central Minnesota. Some of the showers from the leftover storms produced gusty winds and eventually caused wind damage from Waseca, eastward to Owatonna and Ellendale. There were a couple of reports of measured wind gusts of 56 knots from trained spotters and wind sensors from the Department of Transportation. Some of the damage included a large grain bin blown over and a large tree that was blown down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A shed's roof was blown off and an empty grain bin was blown over. The damage occurred 2 miles southwest of Waseca.

Wind shear values were quite high as a 50-80 knot 500 hPa jet moved overhead. At the surface, a strong low-pressure system moved from north-central Iowa, north-northeastward along this frontal boundary. Scattered thunderstorms developed in the afternoon in the warm sector of the storm system. Some rotation was noted as the storms propagated northeast. However, most of the reports were rotating wall clouds vs. funnel clouds or tornadoes. There was a report of large hail near Waterville as this storm moved across the area. The strongest storm moved across Dakota County and produced several reports of large hail; the largest was 2.5 inches in diameter which fell near Inver Grove Heights. Almost all of the reports came from social media pictures and relayed to the NWS. ||By the early evening, most of the storms weakened and moved off into west-central Wisconsin.

During the evening of Sunday, March 14th, a very large storm system began to move out into the plains with a broad circulation across most of the Northern Plains/Upper Midwest. The surface low was centered across Kansas with several weaker shortwaves moving around the broad circulation through Monday night, March 15th. Initially, very dry air below 15,000 feet was noted from Sunday evening and Monday morning soundings at the Chanhassen NWS. This kept most of the precipitation from reaching the ground until the atmosphere started to saturate from the continuous feed of mid-level moisture.||Surface observations at Fairmont, Minnesota reported light precipitation around 1 AM CST. This area of precipitation slowly spread northward during the morning, reaching Redwood Falls, New Ulm, Mankato, and Waseca around 330 AM CST. The precipitation didn't reach the southwest metro area until 9-10 AM CST as the dry easterly flow continued. ||As the system began to pivot and the deeper moisture moved northward, the precipitation ended in portions of southwest Minnesota. However, one continuous feed of mid-level moisture across southeast Minnesota, and into south-central Minnesota kept a steady area of moderate to heavy snowfall. This area of heavier precipitation was able to move as far north as the southern Twin Cities metro area during the mid-late afternoon. However, most of the forcing with this system was weakening and the precipitation started to end. By the early evening, the steadier snowfall had ended with some lingering light snow across east-central and central Minnesota. ||The heavier snowfall occurred in a 25-50 mile band from New Ulm, southeast and east toward Mankato, Waseca, and Owatonna. The heaviest was reported around Mankato where there were a couple of reports of 10 to 11 inches in a 12 hour period. Another 10 to 11 inch report was near Nicollet, Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several sources of weather observations indicated 6 to 8 inches of snow fell across Le Sueur County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large slow-moving area of low pressure wandered from Colorado to eastern South Dakota over a two-day period. Precipitation circulated back into northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota, which started as a period of rainfall prior to evening on March 14. Rain mixed and changed to snow during the late evening and early overnight. Snow became heavy at times during overnight, with snowfall rates up to 2 inches per hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 4.0 inches at Lake Wilson. Travel became hazardous as snow rates increased at night.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abnormally warm temperatures were present across the region leading up to this event with highs in the mid 50s. The warm air near the surface set the stage for a wintry mix followed by heavy wet snow by the evening hours. Additionally, a surface low was deepening as it encroached on the Northland, bringing gusty winds with the Duluth airport reporting a gust of 55 mph just before midnight. The combination of wet, heavy snow clumping to trees and the enhanced winds lead to several downed trees and power outages throughout the area. Snowfall totals were highest across the Arrowhead, ranging from 6 to 11 inches.

Scattered thunderstorms developed across southwest Minnesota and moved rapidly northeast during the morning of Tuesday, April 27th. These thunderstorms produced 1.25 diameter hail north of Trimont, Minnesota before weakening. There was also a report of severe hail northeast of Trimont, but most of the hailstones were less than 3/4 of an inch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotters sent pictures of large hailstones. The hailstones were described as at least half dollar size. The hailstones could have been larger based on the picture.

Scattered thunderstorms moved across southeast Minnesota during the early morning hours of April 27th. Some of these storms dropped hail that ranged in size from nickels up to 1.5 inches. The largest hail fell just southeast of Winona (Winona County). Additional storms developed during the afternoon of the 27th and produced penny sized hail in Byron (Olmsted County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail was reported between Winona and Pickwick.

More widespread and continuous precipitation developed in the late afternoon with light to moderate snowfall around the Alexandria area. The snowfall continued into the evening before ending after midnight. ||There were several reports of snowfall amounts between 4 and 8 inches across northern Stevens, northern Douglas, and northern Todd Counties. The heaviest, with isolated reports higher then 8 inches, was across northern Todd County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several observations across Douglas County, especially over the northwest 1/2 of the county, measured between 5 and 8 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening of Sunday, March 14th, a very large storm system began to move out into the plains with a broad circulation across most of the Northern Plains/Upper Midwest. The surface low was centered across Kansas with several weaker shortwaves moving around the broad circulation through Monday night, March 15th. Initially, very dry air below 15,000 feet was noted from Sunday evening and Monday morning soundings at the Chanhassen NWS. This kept most of the precipitation from reaching the ground until the atmosphere started to saturate from the continuous feed of mid-level moisture.||Surface observations at Fairmont, Minnesota reported light precipitation around 1 AM CST. This area of precipitation slowly spread northward during the morning, reaching Redwood Falls, New Ulm, Mankato, and Waseca around 330 AM CST. The precipitation didn't reach the southwest metro area until 9-10 AM CST as the dry easterly flow continued. ||As the system began to pivot and the deeper moisture moved northward, the precipitation ended in portions of southwest Minnesota. However, one continuous feed of mid-level moisture across southeast Minnesota, and into south-central Minnesota kept a steady area of moderate to heavy snowfall. This area of heavier precipitation was able to move as far north as the southern Twin Cities metro area during the mid-late afternoon. However, most of the forcing with this system was weakening and the precipitation started to end. By the early evening, the steadier snowfall had ended with some lingering light snow across east-central and central Minnesota. ||The heavier snowfall occurred in a 25-50 mile band from New Ulm, southeast and east toward Mankato, Waseca, and Owatonna. The heaviest was reported around Mankato where there were a couple of reports of 10 to 11 inches in a 12 hour period. Another 10 to 11 inch report was near Nicollet, Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several sources of weather observations indicated 3 to 11 inches of snow fell across Blue Earth County, with the heaviest around Mankato.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large slow-moving area of low pressure wandered from Colorado to eastern South Dakota over a two-day period. Precipitation circulated back into northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota, which started as a period of rainfall prior to evening on March 14. Rain mixed and changed to snow during the late evening and early overnight. Snow became heavy at times during overnight, with snowfall rates up to 2 inches per hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall was heavy at times between 0000CST and 0400CST on March 15, resulting in very hazardous travel conditions. A total of 6.0 inches 4 miles south of Worthington was recorded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon of Thursday, January 14th, a weak frontal boundary bisected the eastern half of Minnesota. To the east, warm temperatures remained with the southwest part of Minnesota temperatures started to fall into the 20s. By Thursday evening, an area of low pressure developed across northeast Iowa and intensified as it moved slowly southward across eastern Iowa. ||There was a prolonged period of warm air advection across most of Minnesota. The coldest temperatures remained across the far southwest part of Minnesota where the strongest winds occurred. As temperatures remained near the freezing mark over most of the forecast area during the daytime hours of Thursday/Thursday Night/Friday morning, a lot of the snow either mixed with rain, or snowfall didn't accumulate. Most of the measurable snowfall occurred Thursday night, and the snowfall in the area that had blizzard conditions ranged from 1 to 3 inches. Elsewhere, locally 3 to 5 inches fell from Lake Mille Lacs to the southern Twin Cities to the Iowa border.||Blizzard conditions were felt over mainly the far southwest part of Minnesota where both temperatures fell into the 20s, and wind gusts of 40 to 60 mph occurred. Communities from Montevideo southeast along the Minnesota River Valley, and toward the Iowa border did managed to fall into the upper 20s as snow and strong winds developed Wednesday night. This allowed for blizzard conditions for a small portion of Yellow Medicine, Redwood, Watonwan and Martin Counties. Elsewhere, even as winds gusted to 30 to 50 mph, blizzard conditions were isolated and weren't prolonged. There were numerous road closures in west central to southwest Minnesota, but MnDOT webcams and roads were mainly ice covered vs. snow covered, with whiteout conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The combination of strong winds of 30 to 50 mph, coupled with snow and temperatures falling into the upper 20s, blizzard conditions developed over the western half of Brown County. Conditions improved Thursday morning as temperatures rose above freezing.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong northwesterly jet streak digging through the central Rockies produced intense and deep cyclogenesis across Iowa on January 14 and 15. Initial precipitation swept through the area as rain, but a surge of cold air into the slowing cyclone wrapped snowfall back into southwest Minnesota as winds increased to 35 to 55 mph, resulting in widespread blizzard conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As snow developed and began to accumulate during the evening on January 14, the increase in winds to a sustained 30 to 35 mph, with a few gusts as high as 45 mph at Pipestone (PQN) resulted in widespread near whiteout conditions. Most state roads were closed by early evening on January 14 as plows were pulled due to dangerous conditions, then were opened again during the morning of January 15. Snowfall totaled 6.0 inches at Pipestone and 3.1 inches at Edgerton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm moved across the Upper Midwest on January 14 and 15, 2021 dumping snow and ice on portions of southeast Minnesota. As the precipitation first moved in, freezing rain occurred with ice accumulations of a few hundredths followed by snow with amounts up to 6 inches across Dodge and Olmsted Counties. The highest reported totals were 6.5 inches near Mantorville (Dodge County) and 6 inches in Rochester (Olmsted County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported 5 to 6 inches of snow across much of Dodge County. The highest reported total was 6.5 inches near Mantorville. The snow was preceded by a short period of freezing rain that produced a glaze of ice accumulation on untreated surfaces.

The highest reported totals were 6.5 inches near Mantorville (Dodge County) and 6 inches in Rochester (Olmsted County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported 4 to 6 inches of snow across much of Olmsted County. The highest reported total was 6 inches in Rochester. The snow was preceded by a period of freezing rain that created a glaze of ice accumulation on untreated surfaces such as cars and sidewalks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of Canadian low pressure dropped into the Northern Plains from late Wednesday, January 13th into Friday, January 15th 2021. Snowfall of 1 to 3 inches combined with strong wind gusts of 50 to 65 mph to produce blizzard conditions during the afternoon of the 14th through the morning of the 15th. Travel was greatly affected or impossible. Schools were closed or had delayed starts both Thursday and Friday.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This winter storm had a similar track of a Colorado/Plains Low developing in the Rockies, moving out into the plains, and into the Upper Midwest/Great Lakes region. There was even some evidence of gulf moisture interacting with this storm as low level moisture transport originated over the western Gulf 36 hours prior to the storm moving across the Upper Midwest. In addition to the evidence of deeper moisture, there was an elevated convective instability layer which led to an enhanced area of forcing and banding. This is where locally 6 to 9 inches of snow fell between Willmar and Mora. The heaviest snowfall was across northern Meeker, southeast Stearns, and northwest Sherburne Counties. ||Initially, the focus of the precipitation was along the Minnesota, Iowa border, during the afternoon and evening of Saturday, February 27th. By the late evening, and early morning of Sunday, February 28th, a secondary area of forcing developed across central Minnesota as mentioned from the evidence of elevated convective instability, combined with stronger mid-level frontogenesis and an upper jet axis. This heavier snowfall band developed over eastern Chippewa, Kandiyohi, northern Meeker and southeast Stearns Counties between 1-2 AM CST. This band expanded farther to the northeast but remained nearly stationary for 3 hours. This led to localized snowfall rates of 1-2 inches per hour in a 50-75 mile-wide band. By 5-7 AM, the band shifted southeast and quickly ended shortly after sunrise. Snowfall ratios during this period were approximately between 15-1 to 30-1. Farther to the south of this band, these snowfall ratios were more consistent with a 10-1 average or less as a mixture of rain and snow developed during the onset of the precipitation. ||Some of the higher snowfall totals included:||8.5 inches southeast of Pleasant Lake.|8.2 inches in South Haven.|7.5 inches northeast of Willmar.|7.5 inches north of Kimball.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several reports of snowfall amounts of 5 to 8 inches occurred across Kandiyohi County. The highest measured snowfall was 7.5 inches northeast of Willmar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the first week of February, an Arctic air mass moved southward across the Upper Midwest and continued through the middle of the month. The core of the cold air remained centered across south-central Canada before slowly weakening and moving off to the east after the weekend of February 13th and 14th. Several days of well below normal temperatures, combined with subtle wind speeds, generated periods of wind chill values below -35F.  Before the weekend of February 13th/14th, there was a period around the 7th/8th where wind chill values averaged near -35F around Benson, Willmar, and Alexandria. The bulk of the lowest wind chills, when combined with temperatures dropping to around -20F to -30F, were Saturday night through Sunday morning, February 13th/14th. The strongest wind speeds occurred in the southwest where the pressure gradient was highest. Wind speeds in this area averaged 10 to 20 mph Sunday morning with wind chills near -45F to -50F as temperatures fell to -20F.  Other areas of west-central to south-central Minnesota had similar wind chill values which averaged between -35F and -45F. Farther to the east, where wind speeds were not as strong, but temperatures fell to near -20F Sunday morning, so wind chill values averaged between -35F to -40F, especially early Sunday morning as the sun rose and mixing in the boundary layer allowed for higher wind speeds near the surface. ||A secondary surge of low wind chill values occurred late Sunday and into Monday morning February 15th, especially across west-central Wisconsin where wind speeds were slightly higher based on the tighter pressure gradient. Actual temperatures were much lower and winds were lighter. However, even a slight 5 to 8 mph wind speed caused wind chill values to drop below -35F for several hours. ||Wind chill values were obtained from ASOS, AWOS, MnDOT, and other private weather wind sensors throughout central and southern Minnesota. ||The following are some of the lowest wind chill values during the height of the cold snap:||-50F at Glenwood, Minnesota. |-50F at Morris, Minnesota. |-49F at Alexandria, Minnesota. |-47F at Benson, Minnesota. |-47F at Clara City, Minnesota. |-47F at Sauk Centre, Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several sources of weather sensors provided wind chill values during this prolonged cold snap. The lowest wind chill value of -35F occurred near Milaca at 0000 LST.

Before the weekend of February 13th/14th, there was a period around the 7th/8th where wind chill values averaged near -35F around Benson, Willmar, and Alexandria. The bulk of the lowest wind chills, when combined with temperatures dropping to around -20F to -30F, were Saturday night through Sunday morning, February 13th/14th. The strongest wind speeds occurred in the southwest where the pressure gradient was highest. Wind speeds in this area averaged 10 to 20 mph Sunday morning with wind chills near -45F to -50F as temperatures fell to -20F.  Other areas of west-central to south-central Minnesota had similar wind chill values which averaged between -35F and -45F. Farther to the east, where wind speeds were not as strong, but temperatures fell to near -20F Sunday morning, so wind chill values averaged between -35F to -40F, especially early Sunday morning as the sun rose and mixing in the boundary layer allowed for higher wind speeds near the surface. ||A secondary surge of low wind chill values occurred late Sunday and into Monday morning February 15th, especially across west-central Wisconsin where wind speeds were slightly higher based on the tighter pressure gradient. Actual temperatures were much lower and winds were lighter. However, even a slight 5 to 8 mph wind speed caused wind chill values to drop below -35F for several hours. ||Wind chill values were obtained from ASOS, AWOS, MnDOT, and other private weather wind sensors throughout central and southern Minnesota. ||The following are some of the lowest wind chill values during the height of the cold snap:||-50F at Glenwood, Minnesota. |-50F at Morris, Minnesota. |-49F at Alexandria, Minnesota. |-47F at Benson, Minnesota. |-47F at Clara City, Minnesota. |-47F at Sauk Centre, Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several sources of weather sensors provided wind chill values during this prolonged cold snap. The lowest wind chill value of -41F occurred near St. James at 0600 LST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of storms developed across portions of southeast Minnesota during the afternoon of May 25th as a cold front was moving across the region. Some of the storms produced large hail as they moved across Wabasha County. The largest reported hail was half dollar sized north of Dumfries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail was reported west of Zumbro Falls.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The coldest arctic air of the season settled across northwest and west-central MN. Minimum morning temperatures ranged from minus 25F to minus 48F with wind chills ranging from minus 45F to minus 60F.  Daytime high temperatures remained well below zero in most locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cooperative Observer, AWOS, and Mesonet sensors reported morning low wind chill temperatures of from minus 40F to minus 50F, each day. Air temperatures of minus 40F or colder were recorded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The coldest arctic air of the season settled across northwest and west-central MN. Minimum morning temperatures ranged from minus 25F to minus 48F with wind chills ranging from minus 45F to minus 60F.  Daytime high temperatures remained well below zero in most locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cooperative Observer, AWOS, and Mesonet sensors reported morning low wind chill temperatures of from minus 40F to minus 50F, each day. Air temperatures of minus 40F or colder were recorded. Norris Camp measured low temperatures of minus 48F and minus 47F on the 13th and 14th, respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This winter storm had a similar track of a Colorado/Plains Low developing in the Rockies, moving out into the plains, and into the Upper Midwest/Great Lakes region. There was even some evidence of gulf moisture interacting with this storm as low level moisture transport originated over the western Gulf 36 hours prior to the storm moving across the Upper Midwest. In addition to the evidence of deeper moisture, there was an elevated convective instability layer which led to an enhanced area of forcing and banding. This is where locally 6 to 9 inches of snow fell between Willmar and Mora. The heaviest snowfall was across northern Meeker, southeast Stearns, and northwest Sherburne Counties. ||Initially, the focus of the precipitation was along the Minnesota, Iowa border, during the afternoon and evening of Saturday, February 27th. By the late evening, and early morning of Sunday, February 28th, a secondary area of forcing developed across central Minnesota as mentioned from the evidence of elevated convective instability, combined with stronger mid-level frontogenesis and an upper jet axis. This heavier snowfall band developed over eastern Chippewa, Kandiyohi, northern Meeker and southeast Stearns Counties between 1-2 AM CST. This band expanded farther to the northeast but remained nearly stationary for 3 hours. This led to localized snowfall rates of 1-2 inches per hour in a 50-75 mile-wide band. By 5-7 AM, the band shifted southeast and quickly ended shortly after sunrise. Snowfall ratios during this period were approximately between 15-1 to 30-1. Farther to the south of this band, these snowfall ratios were more consistent with a 10-1 average or less as a mixture of rain and snow developed during the onset of the precipitation. ||Some of the higher snowfall totals included:||8.5 inches southeast of Pleasant Lake.|8.2 inches in South Haven.|7.5 inches northeast of Willmar.|7.5 inches north of Kimball.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several reports of snowfall amounts of 4 to 8 inches occurred across the northern part of Meeker County. The highest measured snowfall was 7 inches east of Atwater.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Precipitation varied from 50 to 75 percent of normal in far southwest Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought conditions continued through February.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense storm system moved across the Upper Midwest on December 15th and produced a rare severe weather outbreak across southeast Minnesota. One person was killed near Rochester (Olmsted County) when a large tree fell on the victim. Twelve tornadoes occurred across parts of Mower, Fillmore, Houston, Dodge, Winona and Wabasha Counties. Eight of these tornadoes were rated EF1 with the others rated EF0.  The tornadoes largely moved through rural areas damaging trees, power lines and farm buildings with the exception of an EF0 tornado that moved through the eastern sections of Preston (Fillmore County) and and EF1 tornado that tracked across Homer (Winona County). In addition to the tornadoes, damaging winds occurred with several reports of wind speeds over 70 mph. The highest recorded wind gusts were 85 mph in Plainview (Wabasha County), 84 mph in Harmony (Fillmore County) and Mabel (Fillmore County) and 83 mph south of Grand Meadow (Mower County). A state of emergency was declared in Fillmore County. The Governor of Minnesota authorized emergency assistance for the following counties: Dodge, Fillmore, Houston, Mower, Olmsted and Wabasha.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This was the fifth tornado of the night in Fillmore County. This EF1 tornado formed south of Rushford and moved to the northeast before dissipating. The tornado destroyed some farm outbuildings and damaged trees along its path.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed across portions of southeast Minnesota during the late afternoon of May 2nd as a cold front moved into the region. Some of these storms produced hail across portions of Winona County. The largest reported hail was penny sized in Lewiston.
Scattered thunderstorms moved across portions of southeast Minnesota during the evening of May 15th. One of these storms dropped some nickel sized hail near Kellogg (Wabasha County).

This winter storm had a similar track of a Colorado/Plains Low developing in the Rockies, moving out into the plains, and into the Upper Midwest/Great Lakes region. There was even some evidence of gulf moisture interacting with this storm as low level moisture transport originated over the western Gulf 36 hours prior to the storm moving across the Upper Midwest. In addition to the evidence of deeper moisture, there was an elevated convective instability layer which led to an enhanced area of forcing and banding. This is where locally 6 to 9 inches of snow fell between Willmar and Mora. The heaviest snowfall was across northern Meeker, southeast Stearns, and northwest Sherburne Counties. ||Initially, the focus of the precipitation was along the Minnesota, Iowa border, during the afternoon and evening of Saturday, February 27th. By the late evening, and early morning of Sunday, February 28th, a secondary area of forcing developed across central Minnesota as mentioned from the evidence of elevated convective instability, combined with stronger mid-level frontogenesis and an upper jet axis. This heavier snowfall band developed over eastern Chippewa, Kandiyohi, northern Meeker and southeast Stearns Counties between 1-2 AM CST. This band expanded farther to the northeast but remained nearly stationary for 3 hours. This led to localized snowfall rates of 1-2 inches per hour in a 50-75 mile-wide band. By 5-7 AM, the band shifted southeast and quickly ended shortly after sunrise. Snowfall ratios during this period were approximately between 15-1 to 30-1. Farther to the south of this band, these snowfall ratios were more consistent with a 10-1 average or less as a mixture of rain and snow developed during the onset of the precipitation. ||Some of the higher snowfall totals included:||8.5 inches southeast of Pleasant Lake.|8.2 inches in South Haven.|7.5 inches northeast of Willmar.|7.5 inches north of Kimball.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several reports of snowfall amounts of 4 to 7 inches occurred across the southern part of Benton County. The highest measured snowfall was 5.8 inches east of St. Cloud.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the first week of February, an Arctic air mass moved southward across the Upper Midwest and continued through the middle of the month. The core of the cold air remained centered across south-central Canada before slowly weakening and moving off to the east after the weekend of February 13th and 14th. Several days of well below normal temperatures, combined with subtle wind speeds, generated periods of wind chill values below -35F.  Before the weekend of February 13th/14th, there was a period around the 7th/8th where wind chill values averaged near -35F around Benson, Willmar, and Alexandria. The bulk of the lowest wind chills, when combined with temperatures dropping to around -20F to -30F, were Saturday night through Sunday morning, February 13th/14th. The strongest wind speeds occurred in the southwest where the pressure gradient was highest. Wind speeds in this area averaged 10 to 20 mph Sunday morning with wind chills near -45F to -50F as temperatures fell to -20F.  Other areas of west-central to south-central Minnesota had similar wind chill values which averaged between -35F and -45F. Farther to the east, where wind speeds were not as strong, but temperatures fell to near -20F Sunday morning, so wind chill values averaged between -35F to -40F, especially early Sunday morning as the sun rose and mixing in the boundary layer allowed for higher wind speeds near the surface. ||A secondary surge of low wind chill values occurred late Sunday and into Monday morning February 15th, especially across west-central Wisconsin where wind speeds were slightly higher based on the tighter pressure gradient. Actual temperatures were much lower and winds were lighter. However, even a slight 5 to 8 mph wind speed caused wind chill values to drop below -35F for several hours. ||Wind chill values were obtained from ASOS, AWOS, MnDOT, and other private weather wind sensors throughout central and southern Minnesota. ||The following are some of the lowest wind chill values during the height of the cold snap:||-50F at Glenwood, Minnesota. |-50F at Morris, Minnesota. |-49F at Alexandria, Minnesota. |-47F at Benson, Minnesota. |-47F at Clara City, Minnesota. |-47F at Sauk Centre, Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several sources of weather sensors provided wind chill values during this prolonged cold snap. The lowest wind chill value between -40F and -42F occurred between 0300 to 0900 LST throughout the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This winter storm had a similar track of a Colorado/Plains Low developing in the Rockies, moving out into the plains, and into the Upper Midwest/Great Lakes region. There was even some evidence of gulf moisture interacting with this storm as low level moisture transport originated over the western Gulf 36 hours prior to the storm moving across the Upper Midwest. In addition to the evidence of deeper moisture, there was an elevated convective instability layer which led to an enhanced area of forcing and banding. This is where locally 6 to 9 inches of snow fell between Willmar and Mora. The heaviest snowfall was across northern Meeker, southeast Stearns, and northwest Sherburne Counties. ||Initially, the focus of the precipitation was along the Minnesota, Iowa border, during the afternoon and evening of Saturday, February 27th. By the late evening, and early morning of Sunday, February 28th, a secondary area of forcing developed across central Minnesota as mentioned from the evidence of elevated convective instability, combined with stronger mid-level frontogenesis and an upper jet axis. This heavier snowfall band developed over eastern Chippewa, Kandiyohi, northern Meeker and southeast Stearns Counties between 1-2 AM CST. This band expanded farther to the northeast but remained nearly stationary for 3 hours. This led to localized snowfall rates of 1-2 inches per hour in a 50-75 mile-wide band. By 5-7 AM, the band shifted southeast and quickly ended shortly after sunrise. Snowfall ratios during this period were approximately between 15-1 to 30-1. Farther to the south of this band, these snowfall ratios were more consistent with a 10-1 average or less as a mixture of rain and snow developed during the onset of the precipitation. ||Some of the higher snowfall totals included:||8.5 inches southeast of Pleasant Lake.|8.2 inches in South Haven.|7.5 inches northeast of Willmar.|7.5 inches north of Kimball.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several reports of snowfall amounts of 4 to 7 inches occurred across the northern part of Sherburne County. The highest measured snowfall was 7.0 inches east of St. Augusta.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There were several low-topped supercells that developed along a warm front across Southern Minnesota Wednesday evening, May 19, 2021. These storms produced 7 short-lived tornadoes and several funnel clouds across southern Minnesota. These tornadoes were weak and most rated EF0. This was a marginal CAPE, marginal wind shear environment with low cloud bases. A strengthening low-level jet led to an increase in low-level wind shear. This was able to produce a few low-topped supercells that were efficient tornado producers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter took video of a brief tornado that touched down in a wooded area and knocked down large branches.  Another spotter was also monitoring the storm. The tornado was also caught on video by a MNDOT camera along I-35 near Clinton Falls, so the timing could be confirmed. A broader view of the storm suggested this tornado had some characteristics similar to that of a satellite tornado just east of a larger tornado.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters took video of a tornado that touched down in an open field and moved northward. It struck a farmstead at the end of its path, with only large branches knocked down per a NWS damage survey.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense storm system moved across the Upper Midwest on December 15th and produced a rare severe weather outbreak across southeast Minnesota. One person was killed near Rochester (Olmsted County) when a large tree fell on the victim. Twelve tornadoes occurred across parts of Mower, Fillmore, Houston, Dodge, Winona and Wabasha Counties. Eight of these tornadoes were rated EF1 with the others rated EF0.  The tornadoes largely moved through rural areas damaging trees, power lines and farm buildings with the exception of an EF0 tornado that moved through the eastern sections of Preston (Fillmore County) and and EF1 tornado that tracked across Homer (Winona County). In addition to the tornadoes, damaging winds occurred with several reports of wind speeds over 70 mph. The highest recorded wind gusts were 85 mph in Plainview (Wabasha County), 84 mph in Harmony (Fillmore County) and Mabel (Fillmore County) and 83 mph south of Grand Meadow (Mower County). A state of emergency was declared in Fillmore County. The Governor of Minnesota authorized emergency assistance for the following counties: Dodge, Fillmore, Houston, Mower, Olmsted and Wabasha.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This EF0 tornado moved across rural portions of northwest Fillmore County west of Chatfield. The tornado produced damage to several farm buildings and trees along its path.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There were several low-topped supercells that developed along a warm front across Southern Minnesota Wednesday evening, May 19, 2021. These storms produced 7 short-lived tornadoes and several funnel clouds across southern Minnesota. These tornadoes were weak and most rated EF0. This was a marginal CAPE, marginal wind shear environment with low cloud bases. A strengthening low-level jet led to an increase in low-level wind shear. This was able to produce a few low-topped supercells that were efficient tornado producers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two storm chasers took video of a narrow tornado.  A damage survey revealed that several trees were broken or blown down. Law enforcement and spotters were also watching the tornado.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous videos, including some from trained spotters, showed a funnel cloud west of Owatonna. Toward the end, it became rope-like and then dissipated. None of the videos were close-up, and no debris cloud was ever noted, so ground contact contact could not be confirmed by video.  Two surveys by local officials and the NWS revealed no damage anywhere. Therefore this has been recorded as a funnel cloud. Videos showed another funnel cloud close to this one, and a separate entry has been made for that funnel.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Late Monday evening, early Tuesday morning, May 25th, a thunderstorm developed across  southern Mille Lacs County and produced wind damage near Milaca at a farm. Damage was confined to one area but was locally severe. A barn door was blown in, a large iron fence was blown down, and a large tree was uprooted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Damage to a local farm was significant as a downburst likely caused an iron fence and a barn door to be blown down. There was also damage to trees and other farming structures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure in northeastern South Dakota helped produce a few thunderstorms in west central Minnesota on Friday evening, May 14. One of them produced a landspout tornado just east of Canby, which was photographed by a storm chaser and verified by a local farmer.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm chaser took photos of a landspout tornado. It was mostly over an open field, but did knock down large branches of a few trees. Part of the circulation moved across a tractor being driven by a local farmer, but the vehicle remained upright. The time was based on the chaser's phone and compared with radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong northeast winds led to waves of 3 to 5 feet along Park Point on July 1. During the afternoon, a woman was playing in the waves with friends and was knocked over by a wave. She was unable to stand back up and was being pulled away from shore and calling for help. Her friends were able to get her back to shore where she was treated by the local fire department and transported to a hospital.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rescuers responded to the report of a woman who was struggling 40 feet from shore and yelling for help off Park Point Beach on the afternoon of the 1st. The 19-year-old woman was playing in the waves with her friends when a wave knocked her over and she was unable to stand back up and being pulled away from shore. When crews arrived the woman���s friends had been able to get her to the water���s edge. The woman was taken off the beach with the assistance of the Duluth Fire Department then transported to a local hospital for treatment.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged dry spell across northeastern Minnesota continued into July with rainfall deficits of 3 to 9 inches observed for the year through the middle of the month. Drought conditions spread across the entirety of northeastern Minnesota through the month with little relief expected as a warm dry pattern looks to remain in place into August.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several large scale complexes of thunderstorms that developed across the Northern Plains during the evening hours of Thursday, June 10th, moved across the Dakotas, and into western Minnesota by Friday morning. Some of the thunderstorms moved quickly across west central Minnesota and produced a few wind gusts of 45 to 50 mph. A severe wind gust of 59 MPH occurred south of Glenwood, Minnesota. By mid-morning, these storms dissipated as they moved east-southeast into southern Minnesota. By late morning, enough instability built across southeast Minnesota where thunderstorms began to redevelop behind an outflow boundary. These storms became severe in Goodhue County and moved quickly east-northeast into west central Wisconsin. There were several reports of downed trees across eastern Goodhue County, and in the city of Red Wing.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were numerous trees blown down around and in the city of Red Wing. Some of the trees caused damage as they landed on power lines, roofs and vehicles. As the storms moved east into Wisconsin, the Red Wing airport had a measured wind gust of 75 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms from the night of June 10th into the 11th dissipated as they moved east-southeast into southern Minnesota. These storms produced an outflow boundary as it moved across south central Minnesota through late morning. A few reports of non-thunderstorm wind gusts and wind damage occurred around Waseca and Owatonna.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down in a golf course northeast of Waseca. These strong winds were associated with an outflow boundary from previous decaying thunderstorms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broken line of thunderstorms moved across portions of southeast Minnesota on May 31st. A few of these storms dropped some hail as they moved across the area. The largest reported hail was nickel sized near West Concord (Dodge County).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following behind the rare December severe weather outbreak, very strong winds buffeted southeast Minnesota from the late evening of December 15th into the morning of the 16th. Wind gusts of 50 to 60 mph were common with a few gusts over 70 mph. The automated weather observing equipment at the Rochester (Olmsted County) airport recorded a wind gust of 77 mph, which was the second highest wind gust on record for this site. In Rochester, the wind caused a fire from a burning transformer to spread to a garage. The high winds caused numerous power outages to occur across the area. The power outages in and around Stewartville (Olmsted County) were so extensive that some outages lasted into December 18th. The Stewartville school district canceled all classes on the 17th because of the lack of power. An elementary school in Rochester also closed on the 17th because of a power outage. A state of emergency was declared in Fillmore County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts of 50 to 60 mph were common across Olmsted County during the late evening of December 15th into the early morning of the 16th. The automated weather observing equipment at the Rochester airport measured a 77 mph gust. This was the second highest gust on record for this site. A garage was heavily damaged by a fire when the winds caused the flames from a burning transformer to spread to the garage. Power outages in and around Stewartville lasted into December 18th. The lack of power and heat on December 17th prompted classes to be canceled in the Stewartville school district and at one Rochester elementary school.

Unseasonably hot weather on the 5th led to many people heading to the Park Point beaches to cool off. During the late afternoon hours, the lake breeze pushed inland turning light westerly winds into dangerous easterly winds of 20 mph that created rip currents in a short amount of time. Four teenagers were caught in a rip current. Three were able to make it back to shore, but one had to be rescued about 100 yards out.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four teenagers were caught in a rip current after conditions quickly changed along Park Point. Temperatures had earlier been in the upper 90s with light westerly winds before the lake breeze pushed inland and dropped temperatures over 30 degrees in 20 minutes as winds turned easterly at about 20 mph. This led to 4 to 6 foot waves at the beach and rip currents. Two women were able to get back to shore while a man attempted to help another teenager when they were separated. That man was able to return to shore by the other teenager was swept 100 yards out by the rip current and was rescued by the Duluth Fire Department and St. Louis County Rescue Squad.

The first storm developed across northwest Le Sueur County and moved east-northeast across southern Scott, and northern le Sueur County. The city of Lonsdale received hail that measured up to 2.5 inches in diameter. Other hail between an inch, and up to 2 inches in diameter fell along the Scott/Le Sueur County border, eastward toward northern Rice and southern Dakota Counties, and into Goodhue County. Some of the storms deviated well southeast across Goodhue County and produced the largest hailstones of 3 inches in diameter near Cannon Falls. Eventually, other storms developed across southern Minnesota, and moved eastward into west central Wisconsin.||There was an estimated 150 houses that were exposed to the 2 hail or greater. An estimate of 1,000 houses were exposed to 1.5 hail or greater. Based on total and partial roof replacement, the total damages to this hailstorm was approximately 5.5 million dollars.
Temperatures fell into the upper 20s to low 30s on the morning of May 27th. Kittson, Roseau, and Lake of the Woods Counties.

A weak cold front dropping through northeastern Minnesota during the afternoon of the 30th led to the development of showers and thunderstorms. A few of the storms produced small hail with one storm along the North Shore producing a few quarter-sized hailstones before weakening.

An area of low pressure tracked through the Central Plains, and brought heavy snow across west central Minnesota from the morning of the 10th through the early morning of the 11th. Snowfall amounts of 3 to 7 inches accumulated.

Thunderstorm clusters formed during the evening hours of June 5th, with several storms producing damaging wind gusts. This episode went through the early morning hours on June 6th. Hail up to one inch was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This gust was measured by the RWIS station located on Highway 171, milepost 1.8.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A late season winter storm moved across the region on March 15th. This storm dumped sleet and snow on portions of southeast Minnesota with accumulations ranging from 4 to 10 inches. Roads became snow covered and slippery and numerous schools canceled classes for the day. Around four dozen weather-related vehicle crashes or incidents occurred in Olmsted County. The highest reported snowfall totals were 10 inches in Rochester (Olmsted County) and 8.5 inches near Kasson (Dodge County), Preston and Spring Valley (Fillmore County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer observers reported 6 to 8 inches of snow across Mower County. Some sleet also occurred at the onset of the storm. The highest reported snowfall total was 8 inches near Lyle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm system produced a band of mixed rain and snow or sleet, which transitioned to heavy snow from late afternoon on the 10th, through the evening and overnight hours into the early morning hours of the 11th.  Heaviest snow lay along a band from central South Dakota, into the MN/ND/SD triple point and west central MN, and through northcentral MN into the Minnesota Arrowhead.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow of from 6 to 9 inches was reported across the county.

Scattered thunderstorms developed along a weak frontal boundary that was nearly stationary across southwest, to east central Minnesota, and over adjacent west central Wisconsin.  Modest instability and enough wind shear allowed for a couple robust storms to develop along this front. One developed near Redwood Falls and dropped quarter size hail in southern Renville County, north of Franklin. Similar thunderstorm cores  that produced quarter size hail near Franklin moved southward into eastern Redwood County but no reports of hail were received. A similar storm developed in the northern suburbs of the Twin Cities and produced quarter size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions persisted across northeastern Minnesota during the month of October. Precipitation varied across the region with western areas from the Brainerd Lakes area northward to International Falls receiving slightly above normal precipitation for the month while areas to the east received below normal precipitation. Much of the region began the month with a mix of D2 Severe Drought and D3 Extreme Drought. As western areas saw a few days of rainfall during the second week of the month, some improvement was noted during the middle of the month with the removal of over half of the D3 Extreme Drought across the region. Some heavier rainfall towards the end of the month in the Brainerd Lakes was observed, but any improvement in the drought category did not occur until the first update in November.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Much of the area was under D2 Severe Drought with an area of D3 Extreme Drought across southeastern third of the northern part of St. Louis County to begin the month. Rainfall for the month ended up around 1 to 2 inches below normal, but around an inch of accumulation during the first two weeks of the month led to a slight reduction in the area of D3 Extreme Drought. Little rainfall was observed over the last half of the month and no further improvements were observed.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The southern part of the county was in D2 Severe Drought to begin the month with a tiny area of D1 Moderate Drought in the far southeastern corner. Rainfall for the month ended up around 1 to 2 inches above normal. Around a half inch of rain fell during the first week of the month which, when combined with rainfall received at the end of September, led to the removal of most of the D2 Severe Drought with the update on the 5th, leaving just the far northern part of the southern part of the county in D2 Severe Drought. Another 1 to 3 inches of rainfall was received over the remainder of the month, with very little additional reduction of the area of D2 Severe Drought.

An area of low pressure working across the Upper Midwest on the 10th was able to pull abnormally high moisture into the region leading to sufficient instability for the development of storms. An upper jet was also over the area contributing to higher than normal amounts of shear as well resulting in some of the storms becoming severe across the region. Isolated reports of large hail and damaging winds were received along the North Shore of Lake Superior along with a rare October tornado that developed over the Boundary Waters Canoe Area Wilderness and tracked northward into Canada.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A search and rescue crew in the area had to take cover near Eagle Mountain due to the large hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged dry spell across northeastern Minnesota continued into July with rainfall deficits of 3 to 9 inches observed for the year through the middle of the month. Drought conditions spread across the entirety of northeastern Minnesota through the month with little relief expected as a warm dry pattern looks to remain in place into August.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall deficits of 3 to 9 inches were observed across Koochiching County by the middle of July. Areas of D2 Severe Drought entered the northwestern corner of the county on July 6 and spread across the entirety the county on July 23.  The northwestern half of the county was placed in D3 Extreme Drought with the July 20 update as the warm and dry pattern persisted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the early morning of July 28th, 2021, a cluster of storms developed near Fargo, North Dakota. These storms moved east-southeast along the moisture gradient that was evident above the boundary layer moving southeast across central Minnesota. As these storms moved into a better environment of wind shear and elevated instability, a few storms became severe in southern Todd County as they moved southward into northwest Stearns County. These storms caused trees to be blown down near Sauk Centre with a measured wind gust of 68 mph at the local MNDOT highway wind sensor near Sauk Centre.  As this system continued to move southeast, a brief surge in the leading edge of the storm led to wind damage near Waterville Township in Le Sueur County.
