During the afternoon of Thursday, September 16th, a cold front laid across west-central, and central Minnesota. Ahead of this cold front, temperatures soared into the upper 80s to lower 90s. A strong mid-level CAP held through most of the afternoon before a line of clouds started to develop along this front. Storms quickly develop once the CAP broke and with ample wind shear and instability, several of the storms produced large hail, with the largest size measured at 2.5 in diameter. These storms weakened by sunset as they moved slowly eastward toward east central Minnesota.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions persisted across northeastern Minnesota during the month of October. Precipitation varied across the region with western areas from the Brainerd Lakes area northward to International Falls receiving slightly above normal precipitation for the month while areas to the east received below normal precipitation. Much of the region began the month with a mix of D2 Severe Drought and D3 Extreme Drought. As western areas saw a few days of rainfall during the second week of the month, some improvement was noted during the middle of the month with the removal of over half of the D3 Extreme Drought across the region. Some heavier rainfall towards the end of the month in the Brainerd Lakes was observed, but any improvement in the drought category did not occur until the first update in November.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The northern part of the county was in D2 Severe Drought to begin the month. Rainfall for the month ended up around a half inch to an inch above normal. Around 1 to 2 inches of rain fell during the first two weeks of the month but no improvement in the drought category was realized. Another inch or so of rainfall was received during the last week of the month with again no changes in drought status observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the late afternoon, and early evening of Thursday, June 17th, thunderstorms began to develop along an outflow boundary from earlier convection across far southern Minnesota. The storms developed in an area of ample instability and wind shear of 40 to 50 knots across southern Minnesota, and into west-central Wisconsin. The first storm developed across northwest Le Sueur County and moved east-northeast across southern Scott, and northern le Sueur County. The city of Lonsdale received hail that measured up to 2.5 inches in diameter. Other hail between an inch, and up to 2 inches in diameter fell along the Scott/Le Sueur County border, eastward toward northern Rice and southern Dakota Counties, and into Goodhue County. Some of the storms deviated well southeast across Goodhue County and produced the largest hailstones of 3 inches in diameter near Cannon Falls. An estimate of 1,000 houses were exposed to 1.5 hail or greater. Based on total and partial roof replacement, the total damages to this hailstorm was approximately 5.5 million dollars.

A few thunderstorms developed during the early evening across eastern Minnesota where a dry boundary layer, combined with ample instability to cause downburst winds. One of these downbursts caused damage in Maple Grove.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree and several larger tree limbs were blown down in Maple Grove, between the hospital and Rice Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several large scale complexes of thunderstorms that developed across the Northern Plains during the evening hours of Thursday, June 10th, moved across the Dakotas, and into western Minnesota by Friday morning. Some of the thunderstorms moved quickly across west central Minnesota and produced a few wind gusts of 45 to 50 mph. A severe wind gust of 59 MPH occurred south of Glenwood, Minnesota. By mid-morning, these storms dissipated as they moved east-southeast into southern Minnesota. By late morning, enough instability built across southeast Minnesota where thunderstorms began to redevelop behind an outflow boundary. These storms became severe in Goodhue County and moved quickly east-northeast into west central Wisconsin. There were several reports of downed trees across eastern Goodhue County, and in the city of Red Wing.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were blown down near Highway 52, south of Cannon Falls.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure working across the Upper Midwest on the 10th was able to pull abnormally high moisture into the region leading to sufficient instability for the development of storms. An upper jet was also over the area contributing to higher than normal amounts of shear as well resulting in some of the storms becoming severe across the region. Isolated reports of large hail and damaging winds were received along the North Shore of Lake Superior along with a rare October tornado that developed over the Boundary Waters Canoe Area Wilderness and tracked northward into Canada.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were down along Ball Club Road from the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple rounds of storms were observed across northeastern Minnesota the afternoon of the 23rd into the early morning hours of the 24th. The first round developed in association with a warm front during the afternoon hours of the 23rd and produced damaging wind gusts leading to downed trees and power lines. The second round developed during the early morning hours of the 24th and led to a few isolated reports of tree damage and a measured gust to 62 mph at the Duluth Airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A report was received via mPING of trees uprooted or snapped.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure lifted a warm front into the Northland during the day on the 26th. Behind this warm front was a moist and unstable airmass and daytime heating led to the development of strong to severe storms over northern Minnesota that moved to the southeast during the evening hours. One of the first storms was a supercell that produced hail 3 inches in diameter. Later storms continued to produce hail and damage to trees as they moved across the region. The Grand Rapids area in particular was hit hard in this event with widespread tree damage across the city. The KDLH radar was inoperable for this event but a high level of service was maintained by utilizing the KMVX and KMPX radars and one minute GOES-16 satellite data.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Photos and video were received of damage along the shore of Jessie Lake. A dock was overturned along with a boat lift. A pontoon boat that was on the boat lift was also overturned.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Twin 3-year-old boys went missing the afternoon of Monday the 9th in to city of Hibbing. Police were dispatched around 5:30PM to search for the boys who were found an hour later in a locked vehicle near their residence. Both boys were unresponsive and in medical distress when found. After being transported to a local hospital, one of the boys was airlifted to a children's specialty hospital in Minneapolis, Minnesota, where he later died. The other boy was treated and released.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Twin 3-year-old boys were reported missing around 5:30PM the afternoon of the 9th in Hibbing, Minnesota. Officers and neighbors searched for the boys who were located around 6:30PM locked inside a vehicle. Both of the boys were unresponsive and in medical distress when found. Air temperatures at the time were in the middle 80s, but inside the car it was in excess of 120F. One of the boys was airlifted to a children's specialty hospital in Minneapolis, Minnesota, where he later died. The other boy was treated and released from a local hospital.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rainfall remained below normal for most of northeastern Minnesota through the month of August keeping much of the region in D2 Severe Drought or higher. Rainfall deficits of 1 to 2 inches for the month were common, although areas from the Brainerd Lakes northward to the International Falls area saw near to above normal precipitation thanks to a more active pattern at the end of the month. However, with year-to-date deficits still in excess of 3 to 6 inches, drought conditions persisted. Additionally, this was the warmest summer on record in Duluth and the lack of rainfall led to Lake Superior falling below normal levels for the first time since 2014.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: D2 Severe Drought crept into the southwestern corner of Pine County on August 10th with D1 Moderate Drought and D0 Abnormally Dry across the remainder of the county. The are of D2 Severe Drought spread across the western half of the county on August 17th. Moderate rainfall over the last few weeks of the month led to conditions improving to D1 Moderate Drought by the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated severe thunderstorms formed during the afternoon, producing heavy impacts as the result of large hail, damaging wind gusts, and several tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Initial report of a tornado on Lower Red Lake, which moved onshore at Red Lake and tracked for less than one mile. The tornado lifted by 515 PM CDT. Damaging downburst winds, heavy rain, and large hail continued to track southeastward with this storm. Damage was reported to the Tribal College, as well as other homes and buildings in the area. A light pole was snapped and several signs were toppled. Several large trees were snapped.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated severe thunderstorms formed during the afternoon, producing heavy impacts as the result of large hail, damaging wind gusts, and several tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado formed near the shore of Lower Red Lake and tracked to the southeast. It crossed MN HWY 1 near the intersection with BIA15 and penetrated deeply into a large evergreen and oak forest. Trees were snapped and debris was tangled, though most debris tracked with the southeast moving storm. The tornado appeared to have dissipated around 522 PM CDT, after crossing BIA18, roughly 6 miles ESE of Redby.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated severe thunderstorms formed during the afternoon, producing heavy impacts as the result of large hail, damaging wind gusts, and several tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado was likely wrapped in downburst winds, heavy rain, and large ail. The tornado crossed several County and Township roads as it tracked to the southeast, with numerous poplar and oak trees snapped along it's path. It appears to have crossed portions of Dark Lake, Whitefish Lake, and Sandy Lake in Nebish and Hagali Townships, before dissipating. near the western edge of Gull Lake, about 4 WNW of Tenstrike.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple rounds of storms were observed across northeastern Minnesota the afternoon of the 23rd into the early morning hours of the 24th. The first round developed in association with a warm front during the afternoon hours of the 23rd and produced damaging wind gusts leading to downed trees and power lines. The second round developed during the early morning hours of the 24th and led to a few isolated reports of tree damage and a measured gust to 62 mph at the Duluth Airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Photos and measurements were received of the downed trees in Whiteside Park. The two largest trees were elms with one uprooted that was 1 foot in diameter with the second being 27 inches in diameter and was completely snapped about 8 feet up. The Blueberry/Arts Fest was ongoing in the park at the time of the storm and the storm caused significant damage to temporary tents and booths along with the items for sale in the tents/booths. The remainder of the festival was subsequently canceled due to the damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure lifted a warm front into the Northland during the day on the 26th. Behind this warm front was a moist and unstable airmass and daytime heating led to the development of strong to severe storms over northern Minnesota that moved to the southeast during the evening hours. One of the first storms was a supercell that produced hail 3 inches in diameter. Later storms continued to produce hail and damage to trees as they moved across the region. The Grand Rapids area in particular was hit hard in this event with widespread tree damage across the city. The KDLH radar was inoperable for this event but a high level of service was maintained by utilizing the KMVX and KMPX radars and one minute GOES-16 satellite data.

Severe drought developed over the southern half of Big Stone County during the first week in July. The drought expanded in west central Minnesota as high temperatures of 6 to 9 degrees above average, combined with little or no rainfall, dried out soils, and stressed vegetation. Lack of significant precipitation continued during the second week of July, causing soil moisture to drop below the 5th percentile. The severe drought continued to expand during the second week of July to include most of Big Stone and Traverse Counties. At least one-fifth of Minnesota���s major summer crops���21% of the corn and 20% of the soybeans���were rated in very poor to poor condition on July 25.

During the early morning of July 28th, 2021, a cluster of storms developed near Fargo, North Dakota. These storms moved east-southeast along the moisture gradient that was evident above the boundary layer moving southeast across central Minnesota. As these storms moved into a better environment of wind shear and elevated instability, a few storms became severe in southern Todd County as they moved southward into northwest Stearns County. These storms caused trees to be blown down near Sauk Centre with a measured wind gust of 68 mph at the local MNDOT highway wind sensor near Sauk Centre.  As this system continued to move southeast, a brief surge in the leading edge of the storm led to wind damage near Waterville Township in Le Sueur County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were blown over, along with approximately 20 feet of corn flattened on the north side of Lake Tetonka.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed near a surface low pressure area in South Dakota and moved east into west central Minnesota. Quarter size hail was observed in and around Wheaton.

A broken line of slow moving showers and storms developed over parts of southeast Minnesota during the late afternoon of July 1st. The activity formed a funnel cloud that was sighted near Le Roy (Mower County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was sighted near Le Roy.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The severe drought that began in June across portions of southeast Minnesota continued through the entire month of July for the southern parts of Mower County. In late July, the Minnesota Department of Agriculture's Rural Finance Authority determined that an emergency existed and made zero interest loans available to farmers whose crops or livestock were suffering from the drought. These loans were available to farmers where a severe drought or worse existed or in contiguous counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The severe drought that began in June continued across the southern portions of Mower County through all of July.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms moved across parts of southeast Minnesota during the late afternoon of July 6th. Winds from these storms blew down trees and branches in Rochester (Olmsted County) and a tree fell on a house in Winona (Winona County) after being uprooted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was uprooted and fell onto a house in Winona.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure working across the Upper Midwest on the 10th was able to pull abnormally high moisture into the region leading to sufficient instability for the development of storms. An upper jet was also over the area contributing to higher than normal amounts of shear as well resulting in some of the storms becoming severe across the region. Isolated reports of large hail and damaging winds were received along the North Shore of Lake Superior along with a rare October tornado that developed over the Boundary Waters Canoe Area Wilderness and tracked northward into Canada.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were downed along County Highway 57 on the north side of Devil Track Lake by the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms moved across parts of southeast Minnesota during the late afternoon of July 6th. Winds from these storms blew down trees and branches in Rochester (Olmsted County) and a tree fell on a house in Winona (Winona County) after being uprooted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from locally heavy rain caused a rock slide to occur southeast of Lake City. Debris from the slide went onto U.S. Highway 61.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms moved across parts of southeast Minnesota during the late afternoon of July 6th. Winds from these storms blew down trees and branches in Rochester (Olmsted County) and a tree fell on a house in Winona (Winona County) after being uprooted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Wabasha, two inches of rain fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moving into the region during the early morning hours and a low-level jet led to the development of elevated thunderstorms during the early morning hours. While the majority of these storms remained below severe limits, one storm during the mid-morning hours did produce 1 inch hail near Lake Winnibigoshish. A second round of storms developed in association with the warm front later in the afternoon and evening hours that produced sporadic 1 inch hail and tree damage.

Early Saturday morning, July 24th, a complex of storms that developed across the Dakotas earlier moved into western Minnesota after midnight. A broken line of storms developed eastward along the leading edge of the storms over west central Minnesota. Most of the storms in central Minnesota were sub-severe with a few wind gusts of 45 to 55 mph from area AWOS/ASOS reports. The Sauk Centre airport had a wind gust of 60 mph as the storms moved across the area, and the Alexandria airport had a wind gust of 49 mph.||There was another line of storms that developed north of the Twin Cities metro area and quickly moved northeast and produced severe wind gusts around Ham Lake and Chisago City. These storms weakened as they moved farther northeast across east central Minnesota and west central Wisconsin. Surface observations near the area reported wind gusts of 36 mph at the Rush City airport, and farther to the west the St. Cloud airport recorded a wind gust of 45 mph. Other privately owned wind sensors reported between 20 to 45 mph across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was blown down east of Ham Lake. Most wind sensors near the area only measured between 35 to 45 mph wind gusts, with the closest ASOS KANE (Anoka Airport) measuring between 15 to 25 mph during the time of the tree damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the morning of July 19, 2021, between 10 and 10:30am CDT, a line of sub-severe thunderstorms caused a canoe with two occupants to capsize. Neither were wearing life jackets and one of the two occupants drowned. The incident occurred on Loon Lake east of Crane Lake, Minnesota, within the Boundary Waters Canoe Area Wilderness (BWCAW). Estimated wind speeds along the line of storms were 25-30 mph based on nearby observations.

Estimated wind speeds along the line of storms were 25-30 mph based on nearby observations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure lifted a warm front into the Northland during the day on the 26th. Behind this warm front was a moist and unstable airmass and daytime heating led to the development of strong to severe storms over northern Minnesota that moved to the southeast during the evening hours. One of the first storms was a supercell that produced hail 3 inches in diameter. Later storms continued to produce hail and damage to trees as they moved across the region. The Grand Rapids area in particular was hit hard in this event with widespread tree damage across the city. The KDLH radar was inoperable for this event but a high level of service was maintained by utilizing the KMVX and KMPX radars and one minute GOES-16 satellite data.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A photo was received of a power pole that was snapped in Wirt.

Severe thunderstorms formed during the late afternoon and early evening in eastern North Dakota, moving into west-central and northwest Minnesota during the mid to late evening. Damaging wind gusts, large hail, and tornadoes were all reported through the course of the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado blew out two walls and the roof of a pole shed, broke several ash and cottonwood trees, and peeled up steel siding and roofing on other farm buildings in one farmstead. It carried and/or spread debris to the east and southeast for up to 500 yards while it tracked to the east. It also tore off some roof trim, shingles, and siding at three other farmsteads along its path.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado snapped the tops off of several ash and cottonwood trees at two farmsteads along its path, and cracked a wooden power pole near its base.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm tracked across the Upper Midwest from December 10th into the 11th. The storm brought snow and strong winds to southeast Minnesota. Snowfall amounts generally range from 4 to 10 inches with a few reports around a foot. Extensive drifting of the snow occurred as gusts of 30 to 40 mph occurred during the early morning hours of the 11th. The highest reported totals were 12 inches in Lake City (Wabasha County) and 11 inches near Kasson (Dodge County) and in Rochester (Olmsted County). Several schools across the area canceled classes for December 10th ahead of the winter storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported that 5 to 12 inches of snow fell across Wabasha County. Extensive drifting of the snow occurred during the early morning of the 11th when north winds gusted into the 30 to 40 mph range. The highest reported snowfall total was 12 inches in Lake City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense storm system moved across the Upper Midwest on December 15th and produced a rare severe weather outbreak across southeast Minnesota. One person was killed near Rochester (Olmsted County) when a large tree fell on the victim. Twelve tornadoes occurred across parts of Mower, Fillmore, Houston, Dodge, Winona and Wabasha Counties. Eight of these tornadoes were rated EF1 with the others rated EF0.  The tornadoes largely moved through rural areas damaging trees, power lines and farm buildings with the exception of an EF0 tornado that moved through the eastern sections of Preston (Fillmore County) and and EF1 tornado that tracked across Homer (Winona County). In addition to the tornadoes, damaging winds occurred with several reports of wind speeds over 70 mph. The highest recorded wind gusts were 85 mph in Plainview (Wabasha County), 84 mph in Harmony (Fillmore County) and Mabel (Fillmore County) and 83 mph south of Grand Meadow (Mower County). A state of emergency was declared in Fillmore County. The Governor of Minnesota authorized emergency assistance for the following counties: Dodge, Fillmore, Houston, Mower, Olmsted and Wabasha.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado originally developed in Worth County, Iowa, moved into Freeborn County, Minnesota before crossing into Mower County west of Lyle. The tornado was only on the ground for about one minute in Mower County as it moved northeast and dissipated. One farm was damaged by the tornado, otherwise, damage was limited to trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unprecedented storm system lifted across the central Plains from December 15 into the early hours of December 16. Kinematic fields were at the top of the climatological distributions, leading to extreme wind shear in the lowest levels. Ahead of the system, record high temperatures with surface dew point values into the 50s and lower 60s surged toward northwest Iowa and resulted in instability not typically found in December.||A serial derecho developed across Nebraska and Kansas during the early afternoon and raced to the northeast toward Iowa and Minnesota, with widespread damaging wind and a few tornadoes. ||As the cold front swung through the area behind the convective line, 60 to 75 mph winds aloft were mixed to the surface and resulted in a few synoptic wind gusts up to hurricane force. Some light wintry precipitation also fell during the transition toward the much colder air, which led to flash freezing as temperatures fell as much as 40 degrees during the overnight hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds reached 40 to 60 mph during the evening and into the early overnight, with a peak wind gust of 77 mph at the Jackson Municipal Airport (KMJQ).

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds briefly reached 40 to 45 mph during the evening, with a peak wind gust of 58 mph at the Windom Municipal Airport (KMWM).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A historic storm, with a severe weather outbreak of tornadoes that occurred across southern Minnesota, and into parts of western Wisconsin, continued to intensify and move northeast across Minnesota Wednesday night. A strong pressure gradient developed in response to the deepening low pressure and produced widespread wind gusts in excess of 50 mph for several hours late Wednesday night and into early Thursday morning. The highest wind gusts associated with the synoptic scale winds was the Redwood Falls airport that measured a wind gust of 78 mph at 11:30 PM. Wind speeds were not as strong across far western Minnesota near Canby, Appleton, Benson and Morris where wind speeds were less than 45 mph. In addition, farther to the north of the Twin Cities area, wind speeds over averaged between 30 and 45 mph, with only a couple of gusts above 50 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were several hours of wind speeds sustained above 45 mph during the late evening, and early morning hours. Several measured wind gusts of 59 mph occurred at Lydia between 100 - 200 AM CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: December brought an onslaught of wintry systems bringing much needed moisture throughout the region. Several records were broken for precipitation during the middle and end of the month. The whole region saw above normal precipitation with the exception of northwestern Koochiching County. The abundance of precipitation lead to a large improvement in the drought status for the region. By the end of the month, no part of the region remained in D3 Extreme Drought. The Arrowhead still remains in D2 Severe Drought as well as most of Koochiching and northern Itasca counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The month began with D2 Severe Drought across the far northern part of northern Cass County and D1 Moderate Drought across the remainder of northern Cass. Throughout the month the area received precipitation of 1.5 to 2.25 inches above the normal. However, the northern section only received 0.75 to 1.5 inches above normal.Despite this, the area of D2 Severe Drought was removed with the update on the 28th leaving a mix of D0 Abnormally Dry and D1 Moderate Drought across the area.

Wind speeds were not as strong across far western Minnesota near Canby, Appleton, Benson and Morris where wind speeds were less than 45 mph. In addition, farther to the north of the Twin Cities area, wind speeds over averaged between 30 and 45 mph, with only a couple of gusts above 50 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A MnDOT wind sensor near Paynesville measured several hours of sustained winds of 45 mph or greater. Farther to the east, wind speeds were less but still averaged around 35 to 40 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Winter Storm that brought over a foot of snow across portions of the southern, and eastern Twin Cities metro area, and into adjacent west-central Wisconsin, was a classic Colorado Low storm track. Winter Storm watches were posted nearly 60 hours in advance of the first snow flakes that fell across southern Minnesota. Warnings were issued Thursday morning and had nearly 24 hours of lead time before the heaviest snowfall began across the state. ||During the morning of Friday, December 10, an area of low pressure had developed across eastern Colorado. By the afternoon, and early evening, the storm moved across Kansas, and into northern Missouri. By Saturday morning, this storm system moved over the Great Lakes region. Light snow started to develop as early as Friday morning across far southern Minnesota. Regional radar noted a very elongated band of higher reflectivity over southern Minnesota that steadily moved northward Friday morning. This band was associated with strong upward mid-level forcing that remained nearly stationary across the southern part of the Twin Cities metro area, and into west central Wisconsin for several hours. As moisture from the main storm system moved northward into southern Minnesota Friday evening, snowfall rates intensified. Locally, snowfall rates were 1 to 2 inches per hour, with isolated higher rates. Snowfall totals by the time it tapered off were approaching 20 inches.  ||Outside of this intense band of snowfall, this storm system brought widespread light snow to the area with accumulations ranging from a few inches up to a foot and a half of fresh snow. The heaviest snowfall was observed from the southwest to eastern Twin Cities metro, with amounts drastically decreasing as you moved into the northern Twin Cities. ||Schools closed early due to anticipated snowfall with several businesses closing early over the south metro. The timing of the event led to numerous travel impacts, as some of the most intense snow fell during the Friday evening commute. Roadways quickly became snow covered, with reduced visibility due to intense snowfall rates also briefly shutting down flights at MSP airport. In addition, to the airport shutting down, there were cancellations as early as Friday morning due to the anticipated snowfall rates during the afternoon and evening. MnDOT reported over 600 accidents across Minnesota during the height of the storm.||Some of the higher snowfall totals that occurred during this event included;||21.0 inches near St. Paul Airport.  |20.0 inches near Lake Elmo.|19.0 inches near Inver Grove Heights.|and several reports of 18.0 inches around Eagan, Prior Lake and Woodbury.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow developed Friday morning and continued for most of the day before tapering off early Saturday morning. Total snowfall amounts across the county averaged between 5 to 8 inches, with the highest totals along the Watonwan, Martin county line. Some moderate snowfall occurred in the morning and afternoon allowing for totals to exceed 5-6 inches by late afternoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system deepened while it tracked from the Dakotas on the 10th into Minnesota and Wisconsin by the morning of the 11th, and then further deepened as it stalled through the 12th. This system brought the first accumulating snow of the season, of an inch or two, as well as strong wind gusts of nearly 50 mph across west central Minnesota. Blizzard conditions were contingent on when and where snow was falling, from the 11th into the 12th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A historic storm, with a severe weather outbreak of tornadoes that occurred across southern Minnesota, and into parts of western Wisconsin, continued to intensify and move northeast across Minnesota Wednesday night. A strong pressure gradient developed in response to the deepening low pressure and produced widespread wind gusts in excess of 50 mph for several hours late Wednesday night and into early Thursday morning. The highest wind gusts associated with the synoptic scale winds was the Redwood Falls airport that measured a wind gust of 78 mph at 11:30 PM. Wind speeds were not as strong across far western Minnesota near Canby, Appleton, Benson and Morris where wind speeds were less than 45 mph. In addition, farther to the north of the Twin Cities area, wind speeds over averaged between 30 and 45 mph, with only a couple of gusts above 50 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Hutchinson airport measured several wind gusts above 50 mph from 1100 PM CST, to 230 AM CST, with a peak wind gust of 51 knots at 1155 PM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unprecedented storm system lifted across the central Plains from December 15 into the early hours of December 16. Kinematic fields were at the top of the climatological distributions, leading to extreme wind shear in the lowest levels. Ahead of the system, record high temperatures with surface dew point values into the 50s and lower 60s surged toward northwest Iowa and resulted in instability not typically found in December.||A serial derecho developed across Nebraska and Kansas during the early afternoon and raced to the northeast toward Iowa and Minnesota, with widespread damaging wind and a few tornadoes. ||As the cold front swung through the area behind the convective line, 60 to 75 mph winds aloft were mixed to the surface and resulted in a few synoptic wind gusts up to hurricane force. Some light wintry precipitation also fell during the transition toward the much colder air, which led to flash freezing as temperatures fell as much as 40 degrees during the overnight hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds reached 40 to 45 mph during the evening, with a peak wind gust of 60 mph at the Quentin Aanenson Field Airport (KLYV). In addition, brief snowfall resulted in visibility restrictions below 1 mile, making travel difficult.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The afternoon of Sunday, December 26th, a storm system moved across the Rockies, and into the Northern Plains and Upper Midwest by Monday morning. This storm caused a swath of heavy snowfall across central Minnesota between Alexandria, Little Falls, Milaca, and Rush City Sunday evening. The heaviest fell in northern Todd and northern Morrison Counties where locally 12-14 inches of snow fell. Farther to the south, snowfall amounts tapered off to around 4-6 inches from Sauk Centre, St. Cloud, Princeton and Forest Lake. Light snowfall amounts also fell as far south as the Twin Cities and parts of west central Wisconsin. Snowfall was the most intense Sunday evening with numerous locations in central Minnesota receiving 6 or more inches of snow before midnight. Snowfall rates were likely in the 1 to 2 inch per hour range Sunday evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several observations across Benton County measured between 5 and 7 inches of snow before it tapered off Monday morning. Some light freezing rain or freezing drizzle also occurred at the end of this event.

Warnings were issued Thursday morning and had nearly 24 hours of lead time before the heaviest snowfall began across the state. ||During the morning of Friday, December 10, an area of low pressure had developed across eastern Colorado. By the afternoon, and early evening, the storm moved across Kansas, and into northern Missouri. By Saturday morning, this storm system moved over the Great Lakes region. Light snow started to develop as early as Friday morning across far southern Minnesota. Regional radar noted a very elongated band of higher reflectivity over southern Minnesota that steadily moved northward Friday morning. This band was associated with strong upward mid-level forcing that remained nearly stationary across the southern part of the Twin Cities metro area, and into west central Wisconsin for several hours. As moisture from the main storm system moved northward into southern Minnesota Friday evening, snowfall rates intensified. Locally, snowfall rates were 1 to 2 inches per hour, with isolated higher rates. Snowfall totals by the time it tapered off were approaching 20 inches.  ||Outside of this intense band of snowfall, this storm system brought widespread light snow to the area with accumulations ranging from a few inches up to a foot and a half of fresh snow. The heaviest snowfall was observed from the southwest to eastern Twin Cities metro, with amounts drastically decreasing as you moved into the northern Twin Cities. ||Schools closed early due to anticipated snowfall with several businesses closing early over the south metro. The timing of the event led to numerous travel impacts, as some of the most intense snow fell during the Friday evening commute. Roadways quickly became snow covered, with reduced visibility due to intense snowfall rates also briefly shutting down flights at MSP airport. In addition, to the airport shutting down, there were cancellations as early as Friday morning due to the anticipated snowfall rates during the afternoon and evening. MnDOT reported over 600 accidents across Minnesota during the height of the storm.||Some of the higher snowfall totals that occurred during this event included;||21.0 inches near St. Paul Airport.  |20.0 inches near Lake Elmo.|19.0 inches near Inver Grove Heights.|and several reports of 18.0 inches around Eagan, Prior Lake and Woodbury.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow developed late Friday morning. A band of heavy snowfall developed during the afternoon and evening with snowfall rates as high as 1-2 inches per hour along the Ramsey, Dakota and Washington county lines. The heaviest snowfall began to taper off late Friday evening. Total snowfall amounts across the county averaged between 12 to 18 inches along the Ramsey, Dakota and Washington county lines, to around 4 inches in the far north. There were a couple of reports of 18-21 inches of snowfall around Woodbury which were the highest totals in this Winter Storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early season Winter Storm moved into the Northern Plains on the afternoon and evening of the 13th, producing widespread and high sustained winds with areas of light snow across portions of southeast ND and west-central MN, with areas of much heavier snow across portions of northwest MN.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds sustained at 40 mph or greater, with gusts to 53 mph, were measured by MNDOT RWIS stations in southern Wilkin and western Grant Counties. This occurred along with very light snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The afternoon of Sunday, December 26th, a storm system moved across the Rockies, and into the Northern Plains and Upper Midwest by Monday morning. This storm caused a swath of heavy snowfall across central Minnesota between Alexandria, Little Falls, Milaca, and Rush City Sunday evening. The heaviest fell in northern Todd and northern Morrison Counties where locally 12-14 inches of snow fell. Farther to the south, snowfall amounts tapered off to around 4-6 inches from Sauk Centre, St. Cloud, Princeton and Forest Lake. Light snowfall amounts also fell as far south as the Twin Cities and parts of west central Wisconsin. Snowfall was the most intense Sunday evening with numerous locations in central Minnesota receiving 6 or more inches of snow before midnight. Snowfall rates were likely in the 1 to 2 inch per hour range Sunday evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several observations across Douglas County measured between 7 and 9 inches of snow before it tapered off Monday morning. Some light freezing rain or freezing drizzle also occurred at the end of this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system developed across the Northern Plains and tracked east through the 6th of December bringing the first winter storm of the season. As the low moved east it continued to intensify and slow down. This brought heavy snowfall to much of northern Minnesota along with a period of blizzard conditions along the North Shore of Lake Superior. Snowfall amounts of 7 to 10 inches were common across the area with higher amounts to 15 to 18 inches. The highest total was from Grand Marais where a storm total of 20.1 inches was recorded. Additionally, the Minnesota Department of Transportation reported hundreds of accidents and spinouts, with 261 crashes reported between 5 PM Saturday and 11 AM Sunday alone.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snowfall fell across southern Cass County and over 6 inches had piled up near Pillager in about 5 hours. Storm totals of 4 to 8 inches were reported when snow ended.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A band of wind-driven snow lifted north and northeastward across Minnesota on the evening of Sunday, December 26th, with the snow intensifying and expanding over the northern half of the state. The snow became especially heavy late in the evening near Brainerd, where a spotter had reported 3.5 inches accumulating in one hour. Multiple waves of heavy snow then paraded over the Brainerd Lakes, Grand Rapids, Duluth, and the North Shore of Lake Superior, eventually winding down during the afternoon of Monday, December 27th. Strong winds off of Lake Superior led to blizzard conditions along the North Shore and into Duluth. A corridor of widespread snow totals of 8-12 inches was observed with the Brainerd Lakes region climbing near 18 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Crow Wing County received some of the highest snowfall totals from this storm with much of the county receiving 10 to 16 inches. The highest total came from the far southwestern corner of the county near St. Mathias where 18 inches was measured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow accumulated across most of west-central and northwest Minnesota in association with a band of strong forcing. There were widespread reports of 8 to 12 inches of snow from the Red River to Bemidji.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Much of the county received from 6 to 12 inches of snow, from the late morning through early afternoon of the 5th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong clipper low pressure system generated high winds across west central Minnesota during the afternoon and evening on the 13th. Wind gusts were measured between 50 and 65 mph.
A strong clipper low pressure system generated high winds across west central Minnesota during the afternoon and evening on the 13th. Wind gusts were measured between 50 and 65 mph.

Drought conditions persisted across much of northeastern Minnesota during the month of November, but improvement was seen as the month progressed. Precipitation varied across the region with the Brainerd Lakes area eastward to the I-35 corridor receiving slightly below normal precipitation for the month while areas to the north received above normal precipitation, especially along the North Shore into eastern Cook County. Much of the region began the month with a mix of D2 Severe Drought and D3 Extreme Drought with areas of D1 Moderate Drought and D0 Abnormally Dry over far southern areas. Rainfall during the first two weeks of the month led to the removal of the areas of D3 Extreme Drought across Koochiching and St. Louis counties with losses of D2 Severe Drought across the region as well. The pattern turned less active overall for the latter half of the month, but above normal rainfall along the North Shore led to further reduction in the area of D3 Extreme Drought with the update on the 30th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The month began with D2 Severe Drought across the entirety of the northern part of the county. Precipitation was near to very slightly above normal for the month, but no changes in the drought status were observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system developed over eastern Colorado early on the 28th and moved northeastward through the day across the Central Plains and into Upper Michigan by sunrise on the 29th. Favorable conditions for heavy snow were in place across northeastern Minnesota with snow beginning first during the morning hours across the Brainerd Lakes area and spreading northeastward into the afternoon. The forward progress of the system was slower than anticipated and areas of lake and terrain enhancement led to higher snowfall totals than expected over parts of the area. Snowfall of 6 to 8 inches was received over much of northeastern Minnesota with heavier amounts in excess of a foot along the North Shore and along parts of the Iron Range before snow ended that evening and early morning hours. A two vehicle collision just west of Ely resulted in one fatality (indirect) and three serious injuries (indirect) due to the snow and ice covered road.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals of 5 to 7 inches were reported across northern Cass County with high reports of 7 inches at both Cass Lake and Federal Dam.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions persisted across much of northeastern Minnesota during the month of November, but improvement was seen as the month progressed. EVENT_NARRATIVE: The month began with D2 Severe Drought across the entirety of the northern part of the county. Rainfall during the last week of October led to the removal of D2 Extreme Drought in the southeastern half of the northern part of the county, being replaced with D1 Moderate Drought with the update on the 2nd. Precipitation over the remainder of the month was near to very slightly above normal with most of it falling during the first two weeks. This led to further reduction in the area of D2 Severe Drought with the update on the 16th, leaving only areas along and north of US 2 in D2 Severe Drought with D1 Moderate Drought across the remainder of the northern part of the county. No additional changes in the drought status were seen over the remainder of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A historic storm, with a severe weather outbreak of tornadoes that occurred across southern Minnesota, and into parts of western Wisconsin, continued to intensify and move northeast across Minnesota Wednesday night. A strong pressure gradient developed in response to the deepening low pressure and produced widespread wind gusts in excess of 50 mph for several hours late Wednesday night and into early Thursday morning. The highest wind gusts associated with the synoptic scale winds was the Redwood Falls airport that measured a wind gust of 78 mph at 11:30 PM. Wind speeds were not as strong across far western Minnesota near Canby, Appleton, Benson and Morris where wind speeds were less than 45 mph. In addition, farther to the north of the Twin Cities area, wind speeds over averaged between 30 and 45 mph, with only a couple of gusts above 50 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Fairmont airport had gusts over 50 mph from 9 PM CST, to 2 AM CST, with a peak wind gust of 61 mph at 1105 PM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first Arctic airmass arrived before the end of the year with temperatures falling into the teens and 20s below zero. Northwest winds averaged around 10-15 mph, and combined with the very cold temperatures, caused wind chill values to average in the -30 to -40F degree range before the end of the year.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few observations across the county indicated temperatures fell into the teens below zero by early evening, and steadily fell through the night. As wind speeds remained around 10 to 15 mph, wind chill values dropped below -35F, with several values near -40F by midnight.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Arctic outbreak brought wind chills as low as 40 below zero. Winds were relatively light; however, air temperatures were frigid.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills fell to -39 degrees at nearby NDAWN Mesonet sites.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first Arctic airmass arrived before the end of the year with temperatures falling into the teens and 20s below zero. Northwest winds averaged around 10-15 mph, and combined with the very cold temperatures, caused wind chill values to average in the -30 to -40F degree range before the end of the year.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few observations across the county indicated temperatures fell into the teens below zero by early evening, and steadily fell through the night. As wind speeds remained around 10 to 15 mph, wind chill values dropped below -35F, with several values near -40F by midnight.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong fall storm system affected much of the Northland on November 11 into the 12th. Warm temperatures ahead of an area of low pressure led to a rain event across the southern part of the area while colder air moving in from the north led to a changeover to snow from the International Falls area southward into the Iron Range area earlier than forecast. This lead to snowfall of 6 to 8 inches from southern Koochiching County into central St. Louis County where a colder pocket of air moved in and kept snow going earlier and longer than anticipated. The snow along the Iron Range led to snow and ice covered roads and multiple wrecks. One wreck near Gilbert resulted in two fatalities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavier than expected snow led to snow and ice covered roads along the Iron Range. This lead to a multi-vehicle accident near Gilbert that resulted in two fatalities (indirect) and at least two injuries (indirect) from one vehicle. The occupants of the other vehicles in the wreck were not injured or had non-life threatening injuries. Snowfall totals of 5 to 7 inches were reported along the Range with the highest report being 6.8 in Chisholm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light snow showers were seen across the region during the day and into the afternoon hours as a warm front approached the area. Snowfall totals were around 2 inches or less with this system. As snow was ending in the late afternoon, roads became icy in central St. Louis County and led to a single vehicle accident that resulted in injuries and a fatality.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A vehicle traveling northbound on US 53 near Eveleth lost control on the icy road and went through the median and into the southbound lanes. One of the people in the vehicle was killed (indirect) with two others in the vehicle sustaining non-life threatening injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure intensified on December 10 as it moved from the lee of the central Rockies into the Great Lakes. A strong frontogenetic band developed ahead of the system, with weak instability present which resulted in a narrow and more intense band across parts of southwest Minnesota. Snowfall from 6 to 11 inches was common along the axis of the band from south central South Dakota toward the Sioux Falls area, and into southwest Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall was moderate to briefly heavy at times between 1200-1700CST, and produced dangerous travel conditions. Snowfall totaled 4.0 inches 8 miles southeast of Hendricks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably strong area of low pressure moved across the Nation's midsection on the 15th into the 16th and brought severe storms, including a tornado outbreak, on the warm side of the system with snow and strong winds on the cold side of the system. While snow totals were less than expected due to storms robbing moisture from this part of the system, a combination of wind and blowing snow led a a period of near-blizzard conditions across parts of the region that hampered travel over the midday hours in some areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall reports of 1 to 3 inches were reported across the northern part of the county. Wind gusts of upwards of 35 mph led to blowing snow and visibilities reduced to under a half mile during the late morning hours of the 16th that led to difficult travel conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A historic storm, with a severe weather outbreak of tornadoes that occurred across southern Minnesota, and into parts of western Wisconsin, continued to intensify and move northeast across Minnesota Wednesday night. A strong pressure gradient developed in response to the deepening low pressure and produced widespread wind gusts in excess of 50 mph for several hours late Wednesday night and into early Thursday morning. The highest wind gusts associated with the synoptic scale winds was the Redwood Falls airport that measured a wind gust of 78 mph at 11:30 PM. Wind speeds were not as strong across far western Minnesota near Canby, Appleton, Benson and Morris where wind speeds were less than 45 mph. In addition, farther to the north of the Twin Cities area, wind speeds over averaged between 30 and 45 mph, with only a couple of gusts above 50 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Minnesota Department of Highways has a wind sensor near Winthrop. This wind sensor measured several gusts above 50 mph from 1100 PM CST, to 230 AM CST, with a peak wind gust of 50 knots at 1130 PM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: December brought an onslaught of wintry systems bringing much needed moisture throughout the region. Several records were broken for precipitation during the middle and end of the month. The whole region saw above normal precipitation with the exception of northwestern Koochiching County. The abundance of precipitation lead to a large improvement in the drought status for the region. By the end of the month, no part of the region remained in D3 Extreme Drought. The Arrowhead still remains in D2 Severe Drought as well as most of Koochiching and northern Itasca counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The month began with D2 Severe Drought across the county. Throughout the month the eastern portions of the county received precipitation of 1.5 to 2.25 inches above the normal. This led to a slight improvement along the east to D1 Moderate Drought by the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions persisted across much of northeastern Minnesota during the month of November, but improvement was seen as the month progressed. Precipitation varied across the region with the Brainerd Lakes area eastward to the I-35 corridor receiving slightly below normal precipitation for the month while areas to the north received above normal precipitation, especially along the North Shore into eastern Cook County. Much of the region began the month with a mix of D2 Severe Drought and D3 Extreme Drought with areas of D1 Moderate Drought and D0 Abnormally Dry over far southern areas. Rainfall during the first two weeks of the month led to the removal of the areas of D3 Extreme Drought across Koochiching and St. Louis counties with losses of D2 Severe Drought across the region as well. The pattern turned less active overall for the latter half of the month, but above normal rainfall along the North Shore led to further reduction in the area of D3 Extreme Drought with the update on the 30th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The month began with D2 Severe Drought across the northern quarter of the county. Rainfall during the last week of October led to the removal of D2 Extreme Drought across the county with the update on the 2nd, leaving a small area of D1 Moderate Drought in the north and D0 Abnormally Dry over much of the county.

Snowfall from 6 to 11 inches was common along the axis of the band from south central South Dakota toward the Sioux Falls area, and into southwest Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall was moderate to heavy at times between 1000-1900CST, and produced treacherous travel conditions. Snowfall totaled 10.6 inches at Windom.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions persisted across much of northeastern Minnesota during the month of November, but improvement was seen as the month progressed. Precipitation varied across the region with the Brainerd Lakes area eastward to the I-35 corridor receiving slightly below normal precipitation for the month while areas to the north received above normal precipitation, especially along the North Shore into eastern Cook County. Much of the region began the month with a mix of D2 Severe Drought and D3 Extreme Drought with areas of D1 Moderate Drought and D0 Abnormally Dry over far southern areas. Rainfall during the first two weeks of the month led to the removal of the areas of D3 Extreme Drought across Koochiching and St. Louis counties with losses of D2 Severe Drought across the region as well. The pattern turned less active overall for the latter half of the month, but above normal rainfall along the North Shore led to further reduction in the area of D3 Extreme Drought with the update on the 30th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The month began with D2 Severe Drought across most of southern St. Louis County along with the far northwest corner of Carlton County. D1 Moderate Drought was observed from the Twin Ports area into most of Carlton County. Precipitation of an inch to an inch and a half was received during the first two weeks of the month leading to a slight reduction in the area of D2 Severe Drought in the southeastern corner of St. Louis County. An overall dry remainder of the month resulted in no additional changes to the drought index.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The month began with D2 Severe Drought across the entirety of the northern part of the county. Rainfall during the last week of October led to the removal of D2 Extreme Drought for areas west of the Mississippi River with the update on the 2nd, leaving D2 Extreme Drought in areas along and east of the Mississippi River. Precipitation of 1 to 2 inches was observed over the month which was slightly above normal for most of the area. However, no additional changes in the drought status were observed over the remainder of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure working across the Upper Midwest on the 10th was able to pull abnormally high moisture into the region leading to sufficient instability for the development of storms. An upper jet was also over the area contributing to higher than normal amounts of shear as well resulting in some of the storms becoming severe across the region. Isolated reports of large hail and damaging winds were received along the North Shore of Lake Superior along with a rare October tornado that developed over the Boundary Waters Canoe Area Wilderness and tracked northward into Canada.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A rare late-season strong tornado caused tree damage|across parts of the Boundary Waters Canoe Area Wilderness in |northern Cook County. The tornado appeared to begin on Alder Lake, |initially causing damage near Campsite #3 where a small swath of |trees were downed, several snapped halfway down their trunks. The |tornado then traveled north causing sporadic damage until just north |of Rocky Lake where there was a more concentrated area of downed |trees roughly 270 yards across. Sporadic tree damage continued until |reaching Clearwater Lake and crossing the lake just west of Campsite |#5, causing significant damage north of Clearwater Lake and north to |the International Border (Mountain Lake). Significant tree damage was|observed along the Border Route Trail, of which passage was made |impossible as the tornado reached its maximum width of around 500 |yards as it moved across the trail. Numerous trees were snapped near |their bases within this area of damage. The coverage of damage was |widespread with satellite imagery depicting over 80% of trees |uprooted, bent, or snapped across its path near the Border Route |Trail. The tornado then crossed the International Border and continued into Northwestern Ontario, Canada. No private property was damaged along the course of the tornado. ||The National Weather Service appreciates the public reports, photos,|and videos of this tornado damage, aerial photographs from the US |Forest Service, and satellite imagery shared by the Northern |Tornadoes Project (Western University in Canada) which all aided in |an accurate assessment of the damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A historic storm, with a severe weather outbreak of tornadoes that occurred across southern Minnesota, and into parts of western Wisconsin, continued to intensify and move northeast across Minnesota Wednesday night. A strong pressure gradient developed in response to the deepening low pressure and produced widespread wind gusts in excess of 50 mph for several hours late Wednesday night and into early Thursday morning. The highest wind gusts associated with the synoptic scale winds was the Redwood Falls airport that measured a wind gust of 78 mph at 11:30 PM. Wind speeds were not as strong across far western Minnesota near Canby, Appleton, Benson and Morris where wind speeds were less than 45 mph. In addition, farther to the north of the Twin Cities area, wind speeds over averaged between 30 and 45 mph, with only a couple of gusts above 50 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several weather sensors measured sustained winds over 45 mph from the late evening, through the early morning. The Faribault airport had a peak wind gust of 51 MPH at 0100 AM CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unprecedented storm system lifted across the central Plains from December 15 into the early hours of December 16. Kinematic fields were at the top of the climatological distributions, leading to extreme wind shear in the lowest levels. Ahead of the system, record high temperatures with surface dew point values into the 50s and lower 60s surged toward northwest Iowa and resulted in instability not typically found in December.||A serial derecho developed across Nebraska and Kansas during the early afternoon and raced to the northeast toward Iowa and Minnesota, with widespread damaging wind and a few tornadoes. ||As the cold front swung through the area behind the convective line, 60 to 75 mph winds aloft were mixed to the surface and resulted in a few synoptic wind gusts up to hurricane force. Some light wintry precipitation also fell during the transition toward the much colder air, which led to flash freezing as temperatures fell as much as 40 degrees during the overnight hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds reached 40 to 46 mph during the evening and into the early overnight, with a peak wind gust of 56 mph at the Slayton Municipal Airport (KDVP). In addition, light snowfall briefly resulted in visibility restrictions below 1 mile, making travel difficult.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The severe drought that began in June across parts of southeast Minnesota came to end  following a period of rain in the middle of August.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The severe drought that began in June across Mower County came to an end in the middle of August following a period of rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rainfall remained below normal for most of northeastern Minnesota through the month of August keeping much of the region in D2 Severe Drought or higher. Rainfall deficits of 1 to 2 inches for the month were common, although areas from the Brainerd Lakes area northward to the International Falls area saw near to above normal precipitation thanks to a more active pattern at the end of the month. However, with year-to-date deficits still in excess of 3 to 6 inches, drought conditions persisted. Additionally, this was the warmest summer on record in Duluth and the lack of rainfall led to Lake Superior falling below normal levels for the first time since 2014.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The northern part of the county began the month with a mix of D3 Extreme Drought across the far northwestern corner of the county with D2 Severe Drought across the remainder. Little rainfall during the first two weeks of the month led to the upgrade of the remainder of the northern part of county to D3 Extreme Drought on the 17th. Moderate to heavy rainfall during the last week of the month brought the monthly rainfall total to near to slightly above normal levels, but with seasonal deficits in excess of 3 to 6, no improvement in the drought was seen.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak wind was measured at a personal weather station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two friends were canoeing on Sand Lake near Britt, Minnesota, on the afternoon of the 12th. Strong northwesterly winds in the wake of a cold front led to waves flooding their canoe. The two men attempted to swim to shore and one was able to reach a small island. The second man didn't reach shore and is presumed to have drowned. Neither man was wearing a life jacket.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two men went canoeing on Sand Lake near Britt, Minnesota, during the afternoon of the 12th. Strong west-northwest winds of 15 to 20 mph with gusts of 25 to 30 mph generated waves on Sand Lake that began to flood the canoe. Both men tried to swim for shore and one made it to an island and was rescued. The other man did not reach land and is presumed to have drowned as they were not wearing life jackets. Rescuers continued to search for the man's body into the weekend.

Rainfall deficits of 1 to 2 inches for the month were common, although areas from the Brainerd Lakes area northward to the International Falls area saw near to above normal precipitation thanks to a more active pattern at the end of the month. However, with year-to-date deficits still in excess of 3 to 6 inches, drought conditions persisted. Additionally, this was the warmest summer on record in Duluth and the lack of rainfall led to Lake Superior falling below normal levels for the first time since 2014.

The dry pattern persisted through the month and D2 Severe Drought spread across the remainder of the area by August 17th and then D3 Extreme Drought by August 24th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wildfire was first detected around 3pm on August 5, 2021, near Greenwood Lake in Lake County. Officials believe the fire was started by a lightning strike in the past that caused a peat bog to smolder. Dry conditions and gusty winds caused the fire to grow in size to around 5,000 acres by the 20th before extreme fire behavior in the following days brought the size to 26,000 acres with zero containment. Numerous structures were damaged or destroyed during this period. Heavy rainfall during the last week of the month kept the size of the fire in check and helped crews reach 37% containment by the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wildfire was first detected around 3pm on August 5, 2021, near Greenwood Lake, which is about 15 miles southwest of Isabella. Officials believe the fire was started by a lightning strike in the past that caused a peat bog to smolder. Strong southerly winds and dry conditions on the 5th caused the fire to quickly grow upscale and was affecting around 1,000 acres by the end of the 5th. The fire doubled in size by the 7th to around 2,000 acres and evacuations were in place near the fire. Numerous aircraft were brought in to fight the fire along with ground crews. The fire grew to around 3,000 acres by the 19th and then to nearly 5,000 acres by the 20th. By the 21st, it had grown to over 9,000 acres in size and led to additional evacuations. The US Forest Service also took the rare step of closing the entirety of the Boundary Waters Canoe Area Wilderness on the 21st for at least a week due to numerous fires in or near the area and the forecast for continued dry and windy conditions. Favorable weather conditions reduced the fire size to 8800 acres on the 22nd before growing to 10,500 acres on the 23rd. The first reports of structures being impacted by the fire were received on the 23rd as well with 14 homes/cabins and 57 outbuildings destroyed. Extreme fire behavior was observed on the 24th with the development of a pyrocumulus cloud and fire-created lightning. The fire increased in size to 19,500 acres on the 24th and increased further to 26,000 acres by the 26th. Cooler temperatures, lighter winds and rainfall arrived on the 27th and 28th allowing crews to reach 14% containment by the morning of the 28th. 1 to 2 of rainfall fell in the area of the fire the night of the 28th into the 29th. Favorable weather conditions the last two days of the month brought containment up to 37% on the 31st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rainfall remained below normal for most of northeastern Minnesota through the month of August keeping much of the region in D2 Severe Drought or higher. Rainfall deficits of 1 to 2 inches for the month were common, although areas from the Brainerd Lakes area northward to the International Falls area saw near to above normal precipitation thanks to a more active pattern at the end of the month. However, with year-to-date deficits still in excess of 3 to 6 inches, drought conditions persisted. EVENT_NARRATIVE: The county began the month with a mix of D3 Extreme Drought across the northwestern half of the county with D2 Severe Drought across the southeastern half. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northeasterly winds of 10 to 20 knots with gusts to 25 to 30 knots led to building waves at the head of Lake Superior. Waves of 3 to 6 feet led to the creation of rip currents along Minnesota Point during the day. A swimmer was caught in a rip current during the afternoon and pulled about 200 yards from shore. They were rescued and taken to a local hospital for exhaustion.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 20-year-old woman was swept 200 yards away from shore at Park Point Beach by a rip current. Duluth Fire Department responded and were able to rescue her. She was sent to the hospital after the rescue in good condition.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm moved over Duluth during the evening of the 28th. While not strong or severe, it did produce lightning that struck one of the steeples of the Hope City Church near downtown Duluth. The steeple exploded from the direct strike, but a fire did not start. In addition to the extensive damage to the steeple, the strike also led to a water main break in front of the church as well, sending a river of water down the adjacent street.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fire crews were dispatched to the Hope City Church at approximately 7:45 PM on the evening of August 28th. One of the steeples of the church had taken a direct strike from a bolt of lightning and exploded, raining debris onto the street below and causing extensive damage to the steeple and the roof of the church that impacted the structural integrity of the building. In addition to the steeple damage, the lighting strike also led to a water main break at the intersection in front of the church. This sent a river of water down North 3rd Avenue East. Fortunately, the lightning strike did not start a fire at the church.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Gusty winds upwards of 60 mph were observed through the mid-morning through early afternoon hours on August 26.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous 1-inch branches were blown down. Gusty winds followed after the morning rain, with a peak wind measured at 48 mph at the nearby Detroit Lakes Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Gusty winds upwards of 60 mph were observed through the mid-morning through early afternoon hours on August 26.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down onto a cabin near Blanche Lake. Times estimated based on nearby wind sensors. Winds were sustained at 35 to 40 mph with gusts to 55 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Gusty winds upwards of 60 mph were observed through the mid-morning through early afternoon hours on August 26.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large, 3 to 5-inch, tree branches snapped by wake-low winds. Report relayed via mPing, with time adjusted based on local wind data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms that originally developed across South Dakota Thursday evening, September 16th, quickly moved eastward over southern Minnesota after midnight. A series of surges along this line caused localized severe wind gusts that produced numerous downed trees and power lines across southern Minnesota. One of the storms produced an EF0 tornado south of Henderson in far southeast Sibley County. ||As this line moved across the southern Metro of the Twin Cities, three tornadoes briefly spun-up leading to multiple power outages, plus major damage to Savage public works building and a large transformer for the city of Savage. In addition, the power outages caused local schools to close temporarily Friday morning. Another area of tornado damage occurred in Apple Valley and Burnsville. Most of this damage was due to trees falling on houses and vehicles.||A 64 mph wind gust was measured by the ASOS at Minneapolis-St. Paul International Airport. At MSP Airport, with the strong winds and possibly brakes not being applied and/or improper chocking, two Sun Country aircraft slid into each other and into jetways H3 and H4, causing fairly significant damage.||Outside the tornadic activity, several sources of private and public weather sensors measured wind speeds of 40 to 55 mph across a wide swath of southern Minnesota. These winds coupled with heavy rainfall, caused numerous downed small trees and large branches across a 25 to 50 mile wide area from Redwood Falls, to St James, then northeast to New Ulm, Shakopee, St. Paul, and into parts of west central Wisconsin.||In addition, near Mankato, MN around 0730 UTC, a 4-year old girl was killed when a tree branch fell on the tent where she and her family were camping. There were  approximately 80,000 people without power during the peak of the severity that moved through the metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A semi-trailer flipped over on I-35 north of Highway 19. There was a wind gust of 58 mph near Elko New Market near the time of the incident.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms moved across southeast Minnesota during the afternoon of September 20th as a cold front moved through. These storms produced a short-lived EF0 tornado across rural Olmsted County northwest of Dover that damaged some farm buildings. The storms also blew down trees in Mantorville (Dodge County) and Dodge Center (Dodge County) and a 64 mph gust occurred near Winona (Winona County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down in Dodge Center.

The city of Savage transformer station also had damage near this intersection, and most of the street lights and signs were damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure working across the Upper Midwest on the 10th was able to pull abnormally high moisture into the region leading to sufficient instability for the development of storms. An upper jet was also over the area contributing to higher than normal amounts of shear as well resulting in some of the storms becoming severe across the region. Isolated reports of large hail and damaging winds were received along the North Shore of Lake Superior along with a rare October tornado that developed over the Boundary Waters Canoe Area Wilderness and tracked northward into Canada.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed onto a power line near the Kabetogama Visitor Center during the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms that originally developed across South Dakota Thursday evening, September 16th, quickly moved eastward over southern Minnesota after midnight. A series of surges along this line caused localized severe wind gusts that produced numerous downed trees and power lines across southern Minnesota. One of the storms produced an EF0 tornado south of Henderson in far southeast Sibley County. ||As this line moved across the southern Metro of the Twin Cities, three tornadoes briefly spun-up leading to multiple power outages, plus major damage to Savage public works building and a large transformer for the city of Savage. In addition, the power outages caused local schools to close temporarily Friday morning. Another area of tornado damage occurred in Apple Valley and Burnsville. Most of this damage was due to trees falling on houses and vehicles.||A 64 mph wind gust was measured by the ASOS at Minneapolis-St. Paul International Airport. At MSP Airport, with the strong winds and possibly brakes not being applied and/or improper chocking, two Sun Country aircraft slid into each other and into jetways H3 and H4, causing fairly significant damage.||Outside the tornadic activity, several sources of private and public weather sensors measured wind speeds of 40 to 55 mph across a wide swath of southern Minnesota. These winds coupled with heavy rainfall, caused numerous downed small trees and large branches across a 25 to 50 mile wide area from Redwood Falls, to St James, then northeast to New Ulm, Shakopee, St. Paul, and into parts of west central Wisconsin.||In addition, near Mankato, MN around 0730 UTC, a 4-year old girl was killed when a tree branch fell on the tent where she and her family were camping. There were  approximately 80,000 people without power during the peak of the severity that moved through the metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF0 tornado touched down near the intersection of Highway 13, and Cliff Road in Burnsville. The path length was approximately 1 mile and most of the damage was due to trees falling on houses and vehicles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions persisted across northeastern Minnesota during the month of October. Precipitation varied across the region with western areas from the Brainerd Lakes area northward to International Falls receiving slightly above normal precipitation for the month while areas to the east received below normal precipitation. Much of the region began the month with a mix of D2 Severe Drought and D3 Extreme Drought. As western areas saw a few days of rainfall during the second week of the month, some improvement was noted during the middle of the month with the removal of over half of the D3 Extreme Drought across the region. Some heavier rainfall towards the end of the month in the Brainerd Lakes was observed, but any improvement in the drought category did not occur until the first update in November.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Much of the area was under D3 Extreme Drought with areas of D2 Severe Drought across the eastern tip of Cook County and far southwestern Lake County to begin the month. Rainfall for the month ended up around 1 to 2 inches below normal, but around an inch of accumulation during the first two weeks of the month led to a slight reduction in the area of D3 Extreme Drought in southwestern Lake County with the update on the 19th. Little rainfall was observed over the last half of the month and no further improvements were observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon of Thursday, September 16th, a cold front laid across west-central, and central Minnesota. Ahead of this cold front, temperatures soared into the upper 80s to lower 90s. A strong mid-level CAP held through most of the afternoon before a line of clouds started to develop along this front. Storms quickly develop once the CAP broke and with ample wind shear and instability, several of the storms produced large hail, with the largest size measured at 2.5 in diameter. These storms weakened by sunset as they moved slowly eastward toward east central Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Radar suggested the hail size was much larger over Lake Mille Lacs, but there were no reports from that area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions persisted across northeastern Minnesota during the month of October. EVENT_NARRATIVE: The western two-thirds of the county was in D3 Extreme Drought with the eastern third in D2 Severe Drought to begin the month. One of the storms produced an EF0 tornado south of Henderson in far southeast Sibley County. ||As this line moved across the southern Metro of the Twin Cities, three tornadoes briefly spun-up leading to multiple power outages, plus major damage to Savage public works building and a large transformer for the city of Savage. In addition, the power outages caused local schools to close temporarily Friday morning. Another area of tornado damage occurred in Apple Valley and Burnsville. Most of this damage was due to trees falling on houses and vehicles.||A 64 mph wind gust was measured by the ASOS at Minneapolis-St. Paul International Airport. At MSP Airport, with the strong winds and possibly brakes not being applied and/or improper chocking, two Sun Country aircraft slid into each other and into jetways H3 and H4, causing fairly significant damage.||Outside the tornadic activity, several sources of private and public weather sensors measured wind speeds of 40 to 55 mph across a wide swath of southern Minnesota. These winds coupled with heavy rainfall, caused numerous downed small trees and large branches across a 25 to 50 mile wide area from Redwood Falls, to St James, then northeast to New Ulm, Shakopee, St. Paul, and into parts of west central Wisconsin.||In addition, near Mankato, MN around 0730 UTC, a 4-year old girl was killed when a tree branch fell on the tent where she and her family were camping. There were  approximately 80,000 people without power during the peak of the severity that moved through the metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A private weather station measured a wind gust of 50 knots near Lydia.
