EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms moved across southeast Minnesota during the afternoon of September 20th as a cold front moved through. These storms produced a short-lived EF0 tornado across rural Olmsted County northwest of Dover that damaged some farm buildings. The storms also blew down trees in Mantorville (Dodge County) and Dodge Center (Dodge County) and a 64 mph gust occurred near Winona (Winona County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF0 tornado moved across rural sections of Olmsted County northwest of Dover. The tornado damaged some buildings and equipment at two farms along its path.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening hours, a line of storms along a boundary moved across west central Minnesota, producing 70 mph winds, locally heavy rainfall, and two tornadoes. The tornadoes did minor damage to three farmsteads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado was on the groud for just shy of one mile causing minor damage to two farmsteads. The first farm had an uprooted tree, tree branches down and sheet metal tossed around the farmyard. The second farm, which was located about one half mile to the northeast, reported damage to roof and walls of a pole barn and large tree branches broken.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening hours, a line of storms along a boundary moved across west central Minnesota, producing 70 mph winds, locally heavy rainfall, and two tornadoes. The tornadoes did minor damage to three farmsteads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tornado damage was first observed to the northeast of the intersection of 620th St and 760th Ave, where a single cottonwood tree in a line of trees was snapped at the base. About a mile to the northeast on 770th Ave, another cottonwood tree had a large branch broken and sporadically scattered leaves and twigs. The tornado then damaged a farmstead where an empty grain bin was removed from the base and lofted, rolled roughly 0.4 miles. Additional metal debris was also noted along the northeast path.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An estimated wind gust of 70 mph caused damage to a barn, including a wall and roof.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of October thunderstorms along a front produced rare tornadoes in west central Minnesota during the afternoon hours resulting in damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado was photographed north of Odessa.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Graceville, MN Fire Department observed a tornado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of October thunderstorms along a front produced rare tornadoes in west central Minnesota during the afternoon hours resulting in damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The first evidence of damage by the tornado was found on the south side of a cornfield at the intersection of Traverse County Highway 9 and 740th St. The tornado tracked almost due north, damaging a farmstead south of the 750th St. A quarter of the roof of a metal pole barn was ripped from the steel rafters and tossed to the west-northwest, damaging a power line. Another pole barn was completely destroyed. The southwest side of the barn had poles lifted entirely out of the ground while the east side walls remained intact. Evidence of the tornado continued a mile north, where downed tree branches and tree debris were noted crossing 760th street. The last signs of the tornado damage were just south of 770th street, where a grain auger and hay trailer were flipped over. The total length was shy of three miles from beginning to end.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of October thunderstorms along a front produced rare tornadoes in west central Minnesota during the afternoon hours resulting in damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An eyewitness observed a tornado touch down in a corn stubble field east of MN Highway 27 and 760th Ave. The tornado tracked north across MN Highway 27, damaging a farmstead. A tree was uprooted and fell toward the house, damaging the deck. Minor shingle damage occurred to both houses on the property. Several farm implements were slid or moved several feet from their original locations. A large machine shed lost a quarter of the roof. Debris from the roof was tossed to the north, northwest about 80 yards. Damage was also evident in a corn crop field north of the farm, ending roughly a half-mile-long path.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system of historic strength led to a variety of high-end weather impacts from the central plains to the Upper Midwest and Great Lakes December 15-16. An unprecedented December severe weather unfolded over portions of Minnesota and Wisconsin Wednesday evening, with the Storm Prediction Center issuing their farthest-north Moderate Risk for the Month of December. A serial derecho moving at 60-70 mph tracked from Kansas to Wisconsin, resulting in over 400 reports of damaging wind and numerous tornadoes. Most of the damage across our area occurred from south-central Minnesota through west-central Wisconsin. The strongest tornado damage occurred in Hartland.||Before the thunderstorms, strong southerly winds led to record high temperature at many sites across the Upper Midwest. Temperatures warmed into the mid to upper 50s across much of central Minnesota and western Wisconsin, and even exceeded 60 degrees across southern Minnesota. The warm temperatures melted what was left of the snowpack from the December 10 winter storm, which led to widespread dense fog through much of the morning and afternoon.||After the thunderstorms, strong gradient winds and widespread wind gusts in excess of 50 mph developed for several hours late Wednesday night and into the overnight hours. The Redwood Falls airport measured a gust of 78 mph at 11:30 PM.||Temperatures quickly fell below freezing across western and central Minnesota Wednesday night, causing rain to change over to a wintry mix of freezing drizzle and snow. Snowfall accumulations of 1-4 inches, along with the strong gusty winds, led to low visibility and travel hazards late Wednesday night into Thursday morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado hit several farms, including one where seven buildings had enough damage so they will be totaled. Several windows of a house were blown out. A machine shed at another farm had several pillars of a large machine shed pulled out of the ground. At the end of the tornado, a home had many shingles removed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough approaching from the west allowed for southerly flow to stream across Lake Superior. The long fetch combined with favorable conditions for the creation of lake-effect snow allowed for localized heavy snow along the North Shore of Lake Superior. Snowfall totals of 4 to 6 inches were reported across southern Cook County with a high report of 6.8 inches just west of Grand Marais.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy lake-effect snowfall led to widespread totals of 4 to 6 inches across southern Cook County with the highest report being 6.8 just west of Grand Marais.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A band of wind-driven snow lifted north and northeastward across Minnesota on the evening of Sunday, December 26th, with the snow intensifying and expanding over the northern half of the state. The snow became especially heavy late in the evening near Brainerd, where a spotter had reported 3.5 inches accumulating in one hour. Multiple waves of heavy snow then paraded over the Brainerd Lakes, Grand Rapids, Duluth, and the North Shore of Lake Superior, eventually winding down during the afternoon of Monday, December 27th. Strong winds off of Lake Superior led to blizzard conditions along the North Shore and into Duluth. A corridor of widespread snow totals of 8-12 inches was observed with the Brainerd Lakes region climbing near 18 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions lasting around 5 hours were seen in the Duluth area during the early morning hours of the 27th. In addition to the blizzard conditions, heavy snowfall also occurred with widespread reports of 6 to 10 inches. A few locations just to the northwest of Duluth received upwards of 11 inches of snow during the storm.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals of 6 to 12 inches were seen across the county with a few reports in excess of 13 inches. The highest total of 14 inches was reported in Finlayson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of storms moved across portions of southeast Minnesota on June 17th. Storms during the early morning hours dropped penny sized hail near Wykoff (Fillmore County). Much stronger storms occurred during the evening and dropped large hail across parts of Wabasha and Winona Counties. Hail up to 2.5 inches in diameter fell near Conception (Wabasha County) with two inch diameter hail falling near Kellogg (Wabasha County).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Arctic airmass settled in across the Northern Plains from the evening of December 28th through the 31st and into the new year (see the January 2022 Storm Data entry for additional details). Wind chill warning criteria was met from the 28th into the 29th under dominant high pressure, and again on the evening of the 31st when extreme cold air was reinforced by a passing cold front, across west central Minnesota. Only slight improvements in conditions were noted during the daytime and between these waves. Some of the coldest wind chill readings during this time include -41 degrees F 2 miles NNW of Big Stone Colony, -40 F 1 mile ESE of Ortonville, and -35 F 3 miles WSW of Wheaton.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado hit one farmstead, knocking down trees and causing some damage to outbuildings. At the next farmstead, it destroyed a machine shed. A nearly full grain bin was dented at the top. The house had part of its roof taken off. Near the end of its path, two irrigation pivots were blown over. It was uncertain whether the tornado moved into Mower County, and so the path was not extended into Mower County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system developed across the Northern Plains and tracked east through the 6th of December bringing the first winter storm of the season. As the low moved east it continued to intensify and slow down. This brought heavy snowfall to much of northern Minnesota along with a period of blizzard conditions along the North Shore of Lake Superior. Snowfall amounts of 7 to 10 inches were common across the area with higher amounts to 15 to 18 inches. The highest total was from Grand Marais where a storm total of 20.1 inches was recorded. Additionally, the Minnesota Department of Transportation reported hundreds of accidents and spinouts, with 261 crashes reported between 5 PM Saturday and 11 AM Sunday alone.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: While most of the county received less than 6 inches of snow, the far southeastern corner of the county received 7 inches of snow from the storm.

Hail up to 2.5 inches in diameter fell near Conception (Wabasha County) with two inch diameter hail falling near Kellogg (Wabasha County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Baseball sized hail fell in Goodview.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system of historic strength led to a variety of high-end weather impacts from the central plains to the Upper Midwest and Great Lakes December 15-16. An unprecedented December severe weather unfolded over portions of Minnesota and Wisconsin Wednesday evening, with the Storm Prediction Center issuing their farthest-north Moderate Risk for the Month of December. A serial derecho moving at 60-70 mph tracked from Kansas to Wisconsin, resulting in over 400 reports of damaging wind and numerous tornadoes. Most of the damage across our area occurred from south-central Minnesota through west-central Wisconsin. The strongest tornado damage occurred in Hartland.||Before the thunderstorms, strong southerly winds led to record high temperature at many sites across the Upper Midwest. Temperatures warmed into the mid to upper 50s across much of central Minnesota and western Wisconsin, and even exceeded 60 degrees across southern Minnesota. The warm temperatures melted what was left of the snowpack from the December 10 winter storm, which led to widespread dense fog through much of the morning and afternoon.||After the thunderstorms, strong gradient winds and widespread wind gusts in excess of 50 mph developed for several hours late Wednesday night and into the overnight hours. The Redwood Falls airport measured a gust of 78 mph at 11:30 PM.||Temperatures quickly fell below freezing across western and central Minnesota Wednesday night, causing rain to change over to a wintry mix of freezing drizzle and snow. Snowfall accumulations of 1-4 inches, along with the strong gusty winds, led to low visibility and travel hazards late Wednesday night into Thursday morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A narrow path of mostly tree damage was found just to the west of SE 89th Ave. An irrigation system was overturned, some farm outbuildings were damaged, and a small shed was tossed.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado developed in Worth County Iowa along 500th Street, initially producing some tree damage (see separate entry from NWS Des Moines).  As it crossed into Freeborn County Minnesota, it displaced and destroyed an empty silo on State Line Road. It then destroyed a cattle barn and a pole crashed through a car. The tornado then continued to produce mostly minor tree damage and moved northeast into southwestern Mower County (see separate entry from NWS La Crosse).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system developed across the Northern Plains and tracked east through the 6th of December bringing the first winter storm of the season. As the low moved east it continued to intensify and slow down. This brought heavy snowfall to much of northern Minnesota along with a period of blizzard conditions along the North Shore of Lake Superior. Snowfall amounts of 7 to 10 inches were common across the area with higher amounts to 15 to 18 inches. The highest total was from Grand Marais where a storm total of 20.1 inches was recorded. Additionally, the Minnesota Department of Transportation reported hundreds of accidents and spinouts, with 261 crashes reported between 5 PM Saturday and 11 AM Sunday alone.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 6 to 12 inches was received across the northern portion of the county.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions lasting almost 12 hours were observed across southern Cook County along the North Shore of Lake Superior. In addition to the blizzard conditions, heavy snowfall blanketed the area in 14 to 18 inches of new snow with upwards of 20 inches just northwest of Grand Marais. Wind gusts near Grand Marais topped out in the 70 to 80 mph range for a period around 6 hours during the morning and mid-day hours. A max gust of 80.1 mph was recorded at 11AM 4 miles west of Grand Marais.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system developed over eastern Colorado early on the 28th and moved northeastward through the day across the Central Plains and into Upper Michigan by sunrise on the 29th. Favorable conditions for heavy snow were in place across northeastern Minnesota with snow beginning first during the morning hours across the Brainerd Lakes area and spreading northeastward into the afternoon. The forward progress of the system was slower than anticipated and areas of lake and terrain enhancement led to higher snowfall totals than expected over parts of the area. Snowfall of 6 to 8 inches was received over much of northeastern Minnesota with heavier amounts in excess of a foot along the North Shore and along parts of the Iron Range before snow ended that evening and early morning hours. A two vehicle collision just west of Ely resulted in one fatality (indirect) and three serious injuries (indirect) due to the snow and ice covered road.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals of 6 to 10 inches were reported across the county with a high report of 10.3 inches on Rainy Lake east of International Falls.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense low pressure system raced northeast from Colorado to southern Minnesota from the morning to the evening of December 15th. As a result, west central Minnesota experienced dense fog, strong wind gusts, up to 2 of snow, freezing rain, and a flash freeze from rapidly dropping temperatures following nearly an inch of rain, from the afternoon of the 15th into the early morning of the 16th. Travel was adversely impacted. ||Further south and east, this system produced a historic and unprecedented severe weather outbreak across Nebraska, Iowa, southern Minnesota, and Wisconsin, with record-setting numbers of tornadoes and significant thunderstorm wind gusts.

The Redwood Falls airport measured a gust of 78 mph at 11:30 PM.||Temperatures quickly fell below freezing across western and central Minnesota Wednesday night, causing rain to change over to a wintry mix of freezing drizzle and snow. Snowfall accumulations of 1-4 inches, along with the strong gusty winds, led to low visibility and travel hazards late Wednesday night into Thursday morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large machine shed at a farm had about 1/4 of its roof blown away and its door blown inward (the door had been closed). A little farther to the north-northwest, 15 trees were broken or toppled. One of the trees fell on a truck. Damage was too scant to determine whether either site had sustained tornadic damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system developed over eastern Colorado early on the 28th and moved northeastward through the day across the Central Plains and into Upper Michigan by sunrise on the 29th. Favorable conditions for heavy snow were in place across northeastern Minnesota with snow beginning first during the morning hours across the Brainerd Lakes area and spreading northeastward into the afternoon. The forward progress of the system was slower than anticipated and areas of lake and terrain enhancement led to higher snowfall totals than expected over parts of the area. Snowfall of 6 to 8 inches was received over much of northeastern Minnesota with heavier amounts in excess of a foot along the North Shore and along parts of the Iron Range before snow ended that evening and early morning hours. A two vehicle collision just west of Ely resulted in one fatality (indirect) and three serious injuries (indirect) due to the snow and ice covered road.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An area of enhanced snowfall set up along the Iron Range during the event leading to totals of 5 to 7 inches with a high report of 8 inches near Celina. A two vehicle collision due to the ice and snow covered roads that occurred along Highway 1 west of Ely resulted in one fatality (indirect) and three serious injuries (indirect).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent below normal precipitation since February 2021 allowed severe drought conditions to develop across parts of southeast Minnesota, including Mower, Fillmore and Houston Counties. These conditions continued into July 2021 for Mower County. Rainfall amounts of 1 to 3 inches during the last part of June allowed soil moisture conditions to improve and brought an end to the severe drought across Fillmore and Houston Counties. A strawberry farm near Mabel (Fillmore County) experienced no production from 3 acres resulting in losses just under $200 thousand.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Persistent below normal precipitation since February 2021 allowed severe drought conditions to develop across Houston County. Rainfall of 2 to 3 inches during the last part of June allowed soil moisture conditions to improve and brought an end to the severe drought.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system of historic strength led to a variety of high-end weather impacts from the central plains to the Upper Midwest and Great Lakes December 15-16. An unprecedented December severe weather unfolded over portions of Minnesota and Wisconsin Wednesday evening, with the Storm Prediction Center issuing their farthest-north Moderate Risk for the Month of December. A serial derecho moving at 60-70 mph tracked from Kansas to Wisconsin, resulting in over 400 reports of damaging wind and numerous tornadoes. Most of the damage across our area occurred from south-central Minnesota through west-central Wisconsin. The strongest tornado damage occurred in Hartland.||Before the thunderstorms, strong southerly winds led to record high temperature at many sites across the Upper Midwest. Temperatures warmed into the mid to upper 50s across much of central Minnesota and western Wisconsin, and even exceeded 60 degrees across southern Minnesota. The warm temperatures melted what was left of the snowpack from the December 10 winter storm, which led to widespread dense fog through much of the morning and afternoon.||After the thunderstorms, strong gradient winds and widespread wind gusts in excess of 50 mph developed for several hours late Wednesday night and into the overnight hours. The Redwood Falls airport measured a gust of 78 mph at 11:30 PM.||Temperatures quickly fell below freezing across western and central Minnesota Wednesday night, causing rain to change over to a wintry mix of freezing drizzle and snow. Snowfall accumulations of 1-4 inches, along with the strong gusty winds, led to low visibility and travel hazards late Wednesday night into Thursday morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A local mesonet weather station measured a wind gusts of 51 knots.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: About one dozen trees were damaged. A storage shed lost a portion of its tin roof. Damage was too scant and scattered to consider this tornadic.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of storms moved across portions of southeast Minnesota on June 17th. Storms during the early morning hours dropped penny sized hail near Wykoff (Fillmore County). Much stronger storms occurred during the evening and dropped large hail across parts of Wabasha and Winona Counties. Hail up to 2.5 inches in diameter fell near Conception (Wabasha County) with two inch diameter hail falling near Kellogg (Wabasha County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to 2.5 inches in diameter fell near Conception.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure was positioned across southern South Dakota for much of the day on the 17th, and this allowed for a band of heavy snow to develop and stall across portions of west central Minnesota. Heavy snow accumulated during this time, of up to 6 inches 2 miles north of Browns Valley. Travel was significantly impacted.

A clipper system slid across the Northland on the 21st bringing widespread snowfall across the region. While this system carried some Pacific moisture with it, overall moisture was pretty limited. However, favorable dynamic conditions with the system allowed for higher snow ratios and higher snowfall than expected. While most areas remained under 6 inches of accumulation with this system, some lake enhancement along the North Shore led to a high report of a foot near Hovland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 6 inches in six hours was reported during the beginning of the storm with upwards of a foot received near Hovland by the time snow ended that evening.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another Arctic outbreak brought wind chills down into the 40s and 50s below zero. Winds were relatively light; however, air temperatures were frigid.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chill values fell as low as -51 degrees during the evening and into the overnight hours as reported by NDAWN Mesonet.

The highest snowfall totals include 8.8 inches 2 miles north of Browns Valley, 6.8 inches 1 mile ESE of Wheaton, and 4.5 inches in Odessa.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Just short of 8 inches of snowfall was measured in seven hours near Kerrick on the 21st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A band of wind-driven snow lifted north and northeastward across Minnesota on the evening of Sunday, December 26th, with the snow intensifying and expanding over the northern half of the state. The snow became especially heavy late in the evening near Brainerd, where a spotter had reported 3.5 inches accumulating in one hour. Multiple waves of heavy snow then paraded over the Brainerd Lakes, Grand Rapids, Duluth, and the North Shore of Lake Superior, eventually winding down during the afternoon of Monday, December 27th. Strong winds off of Lake Superior led to blizzard conditions along the North Shore and into Duluth. A corridor of widespread snow totals of 8-12 inches was observed with the Brainerd Lakes region climbing near 18 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals across the northern part of St. Louis County were generally under 6 inches being on the edge of the system, but the southwestern corner did reach 6 inches 12 hours after snow began.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system of historic strength led to a variety of high-end weather impacts from the central plains to the Upper Midwest and Great Lakes December 15-16. An unprecedented December severe weather unfolded over portions of Minnesota and Wisconsin Wednesday evening, with the Storm Prediction Center issuing their farthest-north Moderate Risk for the Month of December. A serial derecho moving at 60-70 mph tracked from Kansas to Wisconsin, resulting in over 400 reports of damaging wind and numerous tornadoes. Most of the damage across our area occurred from south-central Minnesota through west-central Wisconsin. The strongest tornado damage occurred in Hartland.||Before the thunderstorms, strong southerly winds led to record high temperature at many sites across the Upper Midwest. Temperatures warmed into the mid to upper 50s across much of central Minnesota and western Wisconsin, and even exceeded 60 degrees across southern Minnesota. The warm temperatures melted what was left of the snowpack from the December 10 winter storm, which led to widespread dense fog through much of the morning and afternoon.||After the thunderstorms, strong gradient winds and widespread wind gusts in excess of 50 mph developed for several hours late Wednesday night and into the overnight hours. The Redwood Falls airport measured a gust of 78 mph at 11:30 PM.||Temperatures quickly fell below freezing across western and central Minnesota Wednesday night, causing rain to change over to a wintry mix of freezing drizzle and snow. Snowfall accumulations of 1-4 inches, along with the strong gusty winds, led to low visibility and travel hazards late Wednesday night into Thursday morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The first damage observed was a bent road sign near the tornado origin point. A path of tree and utility pole damage continued to the northeast before ending just south of I-90. Several trees were uprooted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A band of wind-driven snow lifted north and northeastward across Minnesota on the evening of Sunday, December 26th, with the snow intensifying and expanding over the northern half of the state. The snow became especially heavy late in the evening near Brainerd, where a spotter had reported 3.5 inches accumulating in one hour. Multiple waves of heavy snow then paraded over the Brainerd Lakes, Grand Rapids, Duluth, and the North Shore of Lake Superior, eventually winding down during the afternoon of Monday, December 27th. Strong winds off of Lake Superior led to blizzard conditions along the North Shore and into Duluth. A corridor of widespread snow totals of 8-12 inches was observed with the Brainerd Lakes region climbing near 18 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow totals over the northern portion of the county were in the 4 to 7 inch range with the highest report of 7 inches being recorded northwest of Bovey.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably strong area of low pressure moved across the Nation's midsection on the 15th into the 16th and brought severe storms, including a tornado outbreak, on the warm side of the system with snow and strong winds on the cold side of the system. While snow totals were less than expected due to storms robbing moisture from this part of the system, a combination of wind and blowing snow led a a period of near-blizzard conditions across parts of the region that hampered travel over the midday hours in some areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall reports of 1 to 3 inches were reported across the county. Wind gusts of upwards of 40 mph led to blowing snow and visibilities reduced to under a half mile during the midday hours of the 16th that led to difficult travel conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold high pressure moved into the Northland to begin 2022. Overnight lows the morning of the 1st settled into the 20s to 30s below zero across much of northeastern Minnesota. Light winds through the night led to wind chills falling into the 30s and 40s below zero with a few locations dropping into the 50s below zero. Two stations recorded minimum wind chills of -52F near Cass Lake and Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The extreme cold wind chills extended into January 1, 2022.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A fast-moving tornado touched down to the southwest of Hartland and moved into town, damaging numerous buildings, trees, and utility poles. The damage in the southern residential part of town was mainly tree and roof damage. As the tornado tracked into the center of town, more significant damage was observed to buildings along Broadway Street. The most significant damage was to two buildings along Broadway and Johnson in the center of town with significant damage to the walls of low-rise commercial buildings. More tree and roof damage was observed to the northeast with some branches observed along State Highway 13. The tornado dissipated to the northeast in a field with no further damage observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent low level jet ahead of an advancing cold front brought a round of thunderstorms to north-central Minnesota during the early morning hours of the 7th. While most of the storms remained below severe limits, one storm did produce hail to the size of nickels in Cass County.

A wildfire was first detected around 3pm on August 5, 2021, near Greenwood Lake in Lake County. Officials believe the fire was started by a lightning strike in the past that caused a peat bog to smolder. The fire was 37% contained at the start of the month and containment increased to around 49% by the 7th thanks to firefighting efforts and around three-quarters of an inch of rainfall. The middle of the month saw around 3 inches of rainfall which helped get containment up to around 80% by the 20th. At this point, the fire was considered well-managed and evacuees were allowed to return to the area. The fire was largely extinguished by the end of the month outside of a few lingering hot spots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wildfire was first detected around 3pm on August 5, 2021, near Greenwood Lake in Lake County. Officials believe the fire was started by a lightning strike in the past that caused a peat bog to smolder. The fire was 37% contained at the start of the month and containment increased to around 49% by the 7th thanks to firefighting efforts and around three-quarters of an inch of rainfall. The middle of the month saw around 3 inches of rainfall which helped get containment up to around 80% by the 20th. At this point, the fire was considered well-managed and evacuees were allowed to return to the area. The fire was largely extinguished by the end of the month outside of a few lingering hot spots. The fire burned just under 27,000 acres with the worst day being August 23rd when rapid fire growth resulted in the loss of 14 primary structures and 57 outbuildings. Little to no further losses to buildings were sustained thanks to fire suppression efforts. All remaining fire restrictions across National Forest lands and the BWCWA were lifted on September 17th. The only remaining closures to public lands were in the immediate vicinity of the fire by the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense storm system moved across the Upper Midwest on December 15th and produced a rare severe weather outbreak across southeast Minnesota. One person was killed near Rochester (Olmsted County) when a large tree fell on the victim. Twelve tornadoes occurred across parts of Mower, Fillmore, Houston, Dodge, Winona and Wabasha Counties. Eight of these tornadoes were rated EF1 with the others rated EF0.  The tornadoes largely moved through rural areas damaging trees, power lines and farm buildings with the exception of an EF0 tornado that moved through the eastern sections of Preston (Fillmore County) and and EF1 tornado that tracked across Homer (Winona County). In addition to the tornadoes, damaging winds occurred with several reports of wind speeds over 70 mph. The highest recorded wind gusts were 85 mph in Plainview (Wabasha County), 84 mph in Harmony (Fillmore County) and Mabel (Fillmore County) and 83 mph south of Grand Meadow (Mower County). A state of emergency was declared in Fillmore County. The Governor of Minnesota authorized emergency assistance for the following counties: Dodge, Fillmore, Houston, Mower, Olmsted and Wabasha.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One side of a roof was blown off a large commercial building and trees were blown down in a path of wind damage southeast of Grand Meadow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rainfall varied widely across northeastern Minnesota during the month of September with much of the region remaining in D2 Severe Drought or higher. Many areas saw slightly above normal rainfall for the month, but with year-to-date deficits still in excess of 3 to 7 inches, drought conditions persisted. Some slight improvement in conditions was seen during the middle of the month from the Brainerd Lakes area northward to the International Border with the removal of D4 Exceptional Drought and a reduction in the expanse of both D3 Extreme Drought and D2 Severe Drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The southern part of the county began the month in D2 Severe Drought. Rainfall of around an inch fell during the first half of the month and did not put a dent in the yearly deficit. Moderate to heavy rainfall during the week of the 16th-20th totaled almost 2 inches and brought the monthly rainfall total to around a half inch above normal but no improvement in the drought was seen. The pattern turned dry again the last week of the month and no additional changes were observed.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The county began the month in D2 Severe Drought with a sliver of D3 Extreme Drought in the far northeastern corner. Rainfall of around an inch fell during the first half of the month and did not put a dent in the yearly deficit. Moderate to heavy rainfall during the week of the 16th-20th totaled almost 2 inches and brought the monthly rainfall total to around a half inch above normal. With the update on the 21st, the area of D3 Extreme Drought was removed from the county and areas along and south of the Mississippi improved to D1 Moderate Drought as D2 Severe Drought lingered to the north of the Mississippi. The pattern turned dry again the last week of the month and no additional changes were observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front marching across the Northland acted as the catalyst for the development of severe thunderstorms. Storms were initially delayed due to a lingering stratus deck but once convective initiation took hold of the region, the strong ingredients in place lead to numerous severe storms across northeastern Minnesota that produced damaging winds and hail as large as 3 inches in diameter.

The home's electrical system was heavily damaged along with heavy damage to the exterior of the home. Firefighters were on scene for about 3 hours surveying the extent of the damage.

The Duluth area and areas immediately adjacent to the lake in St. Louis County and the central third of Carlton County were in D2 Severe Drought. However, a slight reduction of the expanse of D3 Extreme Drought was seen across both southern St. Louis and northern Carlton counties. Moderate to heavy rainfall during the week of the 14th-20th totaled almost 3 inches and brought the monthly rainfall total to around a tenth of an inch above normal. The September 21st update of the drought monitor saw improvement with the removal of D3 Extreme Drought from Carlton County and just a sliver of D2 Extreme Drought in the northwestern corner. Southern St. Louis saw a reduction in area of the D3 Extreme Drought and D2 Severe Drought with the Duluth area even moving to D1 Moderate Drought. The pattern turned dry again the last week of the month and no additional changes were observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong northwesterly winds were observed behind a departing cold front along the North Shore of Lake Superior the night of September 20-21. This wind direction coupled with the terrain along the North Shore can lead to katabatic wind events in this area. Winds gusted to 40 mph or greater at the Bay of Grand Marais for around 6 hours with a peak gust of 65 mph recorded by a personal weather station near Grand Marais. Reports of power outages were received in the Schroeder and Lutsen areas as well as in Grand Portage. This event did extend into Lake County, but no damage was reported with a maximum wind gust of 51 mph at the Silver Bay harbor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter 4 miles west of Grand Marais recorded a max gust of 65 mph during the katabatic wind event just before 2AM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense storm system moved across the Upper Midwest on December 15th and produced a rare severe weather outbreak across southeast Minnesota. One person was killed near Rochester (Olmsted County) when a large tree fell on the victim. Twelve tornadoes occurred across parts of Mower, Fillmore, Houston, Dodge, Winona and Wabasha Counties. Eight of these tornadoes were rated EF1 with the others rated EF0.  The tornadoes largely moved through rural areas damaging trees, power lines and farm buildings with the exception of an EF0 tornado that moved through the eastern sections of Preston (Fillmore County) and and EF1 tornado that tracked across Homer (Winona County). In addition to the tornadoes, damaging winds occurred with several reports of wind speeds over 70 mph. The highest recorded wind gusts were 85 mph in Plainview (Wabasha County), 84 mph in Harmony (Fillmore County) and Mabel (Fillmore County) and 83 mph south of Grand Meadow (Mower County). A state of emergency was declared in Fillmore County. The Governor of Minnesota authorized emergency assistance for the following counties: Dodge, Fillmore, Houston, Mower, Olmsted and Wabasha.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One person was killed east of Rochester when a 40 foot pine tree was snapped off, fell and landed on the individual and his pickup truck.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the early morning of Tuesday, August 24th, a broken line of storms developed across North Dakota and rapidly moved east-southeast toward west-central Minnesota. There were several surges along the broken line of storms that produced severe wind gusts and damaging winds. A few large hailstones also occurred within this line of storms. As the storms moved eastward, the severity lowered with wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was blown down on Highway 7, west of Hutchinson.

A thunderstorm complex that developed across northern South Dakota during the early morning of Thursday, August 26th, moved eastward into Minnesota and toward west-central Wisconsin. Initially, there were numerous reports of severe wind gusts of 60 to 75 mph across northeast South Dakota before the storms moved into Minnesota. Early wind reports across Yellow Medicine and Lac Qui Parle Counties, were between 50  and 55 mph. Two reports of 57 mph winds occurred along the Minnesota/South Dakota border at the MnDOT site south of Marietta along Highway 212. This line of storms started to break up as it moved across western Minnesota, but a few stronger cells produced severe wind gusts near Benson and Appleton. ||As the storms continued to trek eastward, a secondary bow-echo type structure developed between Redwood Falls, and Hutchinson. There were several reports of wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph. The intensity of the storm remained strong as it moved into the Twin Cities metro area and produced some wind damage and wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph. There were a few reports of large trees blown down with a couple measured severe wind gusts. The majority of the damage was minor with a few tree branches blown down across the western part of the Twin Cities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 12 inch diameter tree was snapped at its base north of Waconia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed along an increasing thermal gradient across western Minnesota during the afternoon of Friday, August 20th. These storms congealed into small bow segments that produced severe wind gusts near Alexandria, and two brief tornadoes. This area of storms continued to progress eastward during the evening and produced a few wind gusts of 30 to 40 mph with the stronger cells.

Several storms developed during the afternoon and evening across central and southern Minnesota. Most of the storms were non-severe and producing frequent lightning and gusty winds. One storm that developed southwest of Mankato developed a strong core and dumped up to 2 inch size hail around the Mankato area Sunday evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was a social media picture of half dollar size hail in Mankato.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Despite weaker pre-storm deep-layer shear on the order of 25-30 knots and fairly narrow instability profiles of 1000-1500 J/kg, modifications on the storm scale and an increase in the low-level jet improved the environment enough to support several long-lived supercell clusters along a weak frontal boundary.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts knocked down several 8 inch diameter tree limbs. Property damage costs are a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An elevated supercell storm developed along a mid-level frontal boundary as a weak wave helped to increase and focus the low-level jet. With fairly weak low-level stability, severe winds easily reached the surface along the track of the storm as it transitioned to a linear structure.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts downed a few trees in Ivanhoe. Property damage costs are a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unstable warm sector with MUCAPE up to 2000-2500 J/kg developed during the afternoon between an advancing cold front across southeast South Dakota, and a gangly warm front extending into southwest Minnesota. An approaching weak impulse spurred rapid development of multiple rounds of storms near the warm front, initially discrete, with upscale growth into short bowing segments. Secondary development occurred during the evening along the cold front, which evolved into a slow-moving linear system with hail, wind, and heavy rainfall with shear vectors increasingly parallel to the boundary.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A narrow path of crop damage, possibly related to a miso vortex within a broader wind field, was identified using Sentinel satellite imagery.

The tornado caused damage to crops as it moved north-northeast over open farmland. Sentinel satellite imagery revealed a possible brief lift around 230th Street.  The tornado dissipated along 440th Avenue, one-half mile south of 210th St.  The average path width was 20 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms that developed across South Dakota early Saturday morning, August 28th, quickly moved into west central Minnesota and produced up to 3 diameter hail in Swift County. These storms congealed into a fast moving bow-echo that surged across parts of central Minnesota, between Willmar, St. Cloud and Mora. Several areas received very large hail, severe wind gusts and straight-line winds, and there was one tornado south of Albany. As the storms moved over east-central Minnesota, the severity of the storms lessened and only produced isolated wind damage around Milaca. The worst damaged areas were near Glenwood, Sauk Centre, and north and east of St. Cloud where numerous trees were blown down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were multiple vehicles damaged with windows broken. In addition, siding on houses was damaged and numerous windows were broken to houses and outbuildings in and around Popple Creek.

An isolated supercell developed across south central South Dakota late evening along an advancing cold frontal boundary. With appreciable DCAPE and deep-layer effective shear, upscale growth occurred rapidly with the development of widespread damaging winds, which spread out of eastern South Dakota into Southwest Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A hoop barn was damaged by thunderstorm wind gusts. Property damage costs are a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A secondary wave of severe thunderstorms developed over southwest Minnesota by the late afternoon of Saturday, August 28th. This area of storms formed a bow-echo that moved from Springfield, east-northeast toward New Ulm, Mankato, and Le Center. The most intense part of the bow-echo occurred at the onset that moved across Brown and Watonwan counties. Several tornadoes were documented. Thousands of acres of corn was flattened due to sustained intense winds. As the storm progressed northeast, the bow-echo segment became disorganized south of the Twin Cities metro area. Another wave of storms that originated over southwest Minnesota during the evening, caused strong winds in Brown County but no damage was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A narrow path of crop damage, possibly related to a misovortex within a broader wind field, was identified using Sentinel satellite imagery. It was not possible to correlate the crop damage with any radar circulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the late evening of Thursday, August 5th, a cluster of thunderstorms developed across the eastern Twin Cities metro area and produced isolated hail reports from Woodbury, southward to Cottage Grove. Most of the hail was sub-severe but a couple of reports of quarter size hail were noted. A severe downburst also occurred late Thursday evening as a large tree was blown down along with a pontoon boat flipped.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A public report of quarter size hail relayed from a local TV Meteorologist.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A pontoon boat was flipped over and docks were damaged on Lake Minnewashta. In addition, several large trees were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Temperatures averaged fairly close to normal during July, but precipitation from 50 to 75 percent of normal allowed for the expansion and degradation of drought conditions across southwest Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought developed early in the month, but precipitation less than 50 percent of normal at Tracy and Marshall along with temperatures averaging around 2 degrees above normal for the month brought drought to extreme (D3) conditions by the end of July. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Temperatures were slightly above normal during the month. The spatial and temporal distribution of rainfall, with more widespread heavier amounts toward the end of the month, defined drought trends. Overall, conditions improved by month's end.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought continued most of August, before 3 to 5 inches of rainfall from August 21 to August 29 improved conditions to moderate (D1) drought. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Temperatures were slightly above normal during the month. The spatial and temporal distribution of rainfall, with more widespread heavier amounts toward the end of the month, defined drought trends. Overall, conditions improved by month's end.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme (D3) drought developed in early August, but conditions returned to severe (D2) with 4 to 6 inches of rainfall between August 21 and August 29. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated supercell developed across south central South Dakota late evening along an advancing cold frontal boundary. With appreciable DCAPE and deep-layer effective shear, upscale growth occurred rapidly with the development of widespread damaging winds, which spread out of eastern South Dakota into Southwest Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts were measured by Minnesota RWIS site MN006 located 1 mile west southwest of Beaver Creek. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense storm system moved across the Upper Midwest on December 15th and produced a rare severe weather outbreak across southeast Minnesota. One person was killed near Rochester (Olmsted County) when a large tree fell on the victim. Twelve tornadoes occurred across parts of Mower, Fillmore, Houston, Dodge, Winona and Wabasha Counties. Eight of these tornadoes were rated EF1 with the others rated EF0.  The tornadoes largely moved through rural areas damaging trees, power lines and farm buildings with the exception of an EF0 tornado that moved through the eastern sections of Preston (Fillmore County) and and EF1 tornado that tracked across Homer (Winona County). In addition to the tornadoes, damaging winds occurred with several reports of wind speeds over 70 mph. The highest recorded wind gusts were 85 mph in Plainview (Wabasha County), 84 mph in Harmony (Fillmore County) and Mabel (Fillmore County) and 83 mph south of Grand Meadow (Mower County). A state of emergency was declared in Fillmore County. The Governor of Minnesota authorized emergency assistance for the following counties: Dodge, Fillmore, Houston, Mower, Olmsted and Wabasha.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wooden power poles were snapped north of Washington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unstable warm sector with MUCAPE up to 2000-2500 J/kg developed during the afternoon between an advancing cold front across southeast South Dakota, and a gangly warm front extending into southwest Minnesota. An approaching weak impulse spurred rapid development of multiple rounds of storms near the warm front, initially discrete, with upscale growth into short bowing segments. Secondary development occurred during the evening along the cold front, which evolved into a slow-moving linear system with hail, wind, and heavy rainfall with shear vectors increasingly parallel to the boundary.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A grain bin was damaged by thunderstorm winds one-half mile south of Storden.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Along an earlier outflow-reinforced warm front, thunderstorms developed during the later evening with an increase in the low-level jet, and congealed into a small convective cluster in an environment with up to 2000 J/kg elevated CAPE.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A secondary wave of severe thunderstorms developed over southwest Minnesota by the late afternoon of Saturday, August 28th. This area of storms formed a bow-echo that moved from Springfield, east-northeast toward New Ulm, Mankato, and Le Center. The most intense part of the bow-echo occurred at the onset that moved across Brown and Watonwan counties. Several tornadoes were documented. Thousands of acres of corn was flattened due to sustained intense winds. As the storm progressed northeast, the bow-echo segment became disorganized south of the Twin Cities metro area. Another wave of storms that originated over southwest Minnesota during the evening, caused strong winds in Brown County but no damage was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A small and narrow misocyclone on the leading edge of a storm traveled across corn and bean fields only. Drone video was utilized and an on-site survey was performed. The path was also identified using Sentinel satellite imagery. It was not possible to correlate the crop damage with any radar circulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms that developed across South Dakota early Saturday morning, August 28th, quickly moved into west central Minnesota and produced up to 3 diameter hail in Swift County. These storms congealed into a fast moving bow-echo that surged across parts of central Minnesota, between Willmar, St. Cloud and Mora. Several areas received very large hail, severe wind gusts and straight-line winds, and there was one tornado south of Albany. As the storms moved over east-central Minnesota, the severity of the storms lessened and only produced isolated wind damage around Milaca. The worst damaged areas were near Glenwood, Sauk Centre, and north and east of St. Cloud where numerous trees were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following behind the rare December severe weather outbreak, very strong winds buffeted southeast Minnesota from the late evening of December 15th into the morning of the 16th. Wind gusts of 50 to 60 mph were common with a few gusts over 70 mph. The automated weather observing equipment at the Rochester (Olmsted County) airport recorded a wind gust of 77 mph, which was the second highest wind gust on record for this site. In Rochester, the wind caused a fire from a burning transformer to spread to a garage. The high winds caused numerous power outages to occur across the area. The power outages in and around Stewartville (Olmsted County) were so extensive that some outages lasted into December 18th. The Stewartville school district canceled all classes on the 17th because of the lack of power. An elementary school in Rochester also closed on the 17th because of a power outage. A state of emergency was declared in Fillmore County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts of 50 to 60 mph were common across Dodge County during the late evening of December 15th into the early morning of the 16th. The highest recorded wind gust was 67 mph east of Kasson at a mesonet station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm complex that developed across northern South Dakota during the early morning of Thursday, August 26th, moved eastward into Minnesota and toward west-central Wisconsin. Initially, there were numerous reports of severe wind gusts of 60 to 75 mph across northeast South Dakota before the storms moved into Minnesota. Early wind reports across Yellow Medicine and Lac Qui Parle Counties, were between 50  and 55 mph. Two reports of 57 mph winds occurred along the Minnesota/South Dakota border at the MnDOT site south of Marietta along Highway 212. This line of storms started to break up as it moved across western Minnesota, but a few stronger cells produced severe wind gusts near Benson and Appleton. ||As the storms continued to trek eastward, a secondary bow-echo type structure developed between Redwood Falls, and Hutchinson. There were several reports of wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph. The intensity of the storm remained strong as it moved into the Twin Cities metro area and produced some wind damage and wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph. There were a few reports of large trees blown down with a couple measured severe wind gusts. The majority of the damage was minor with a few tree branches blown down across the western part of the Twin Cities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This was a measured wind gust at the Benson airport. No other damage occurred around the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms that developed across South Dakota early Saturday morning, August 28th, quickly moved into west central Minnesota and produced up to 3 diameter hail in Swift County. These storms congealed into a fast moving bow-echo that surged across parts of central Minnesota, between Willmar, St. Cloud and Mora. Several areas received very large hail, severe wind gusts and straight-line winds, and there was one tornado south of Albany. As the storms moved over east-central Minnesota, the severity of the storms lessened and only produced isolated wind damage around Milaca. The worst damaged areas were near Glenwood, Sauk Centre, and north and east of St. Cloud where numerous trees were blown down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter had a hand held wind sensor that measured the wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A secondary wave of severe thunderstorms developed over southwest Minnesota by the late afternoon of Saturday, August 28th. This area of storms formed a bow-echo that moved from Springfield, east-northeast toward New Ulm, Mankato, and Le Center. The most intense part of the bow-echo occurred at the onset that moved across Brown and Watonwan counties. Several tornadoes were documented. Thousands of acres of corn was flattened due to sustained intense winds. As the storm progressed northeast, the bow-echo segment became disorganized south of the Twin Cities metro area. Another wave of storms that originated over southwest Minnesota during the evening, caused strong winds in Brown County but no damage was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter had a hand held wind sensor that measured 52 knots.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated supercell developed across south central South Dakota late evening along an advancing cold frontal boundary. With appreciable DCAPE and deep-layer effective shear, upscale growth occurred rapidly with the development of widespread damaging winds, which spread out of eastern South Dakota into Southwest Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A hoop barn was destroyed by thunderstorm winds, along with doors and roof ripped off a machine shed. Property damage costs are a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed along an increasing thermal gradient across western Minnesota during the afternoon of Friday, August 20th. These storms congealed into small bow segments that produced severe wind gusts near Alexandria, and two brief tornadoes. This area of storms continued to progress eastward during the evening and produced a few wind gusts of 30 to 40 mph with the stronger cells.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was a brief tornado just southeast of the intersection of Kandiyohi, Pope, and Stearns Counties. It was caught on video and moved across a bean field and road. Although it did not hit any damage indicators, video suggests the tornado was weak.

During the late evening of Thursday, August 5th, a cluster of thunderstorms developed across the eastern Twin Cities metro area and produced isolated hail reports from Woodbury, southward to Cottage Grove. Most of the hail was sub-severe but a couple of reports of quarter size hail were noted. A severe downburst also occurred late Thursday evening as a large tree was blown down along with a pontoon boat flipped.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Most of the hail was pea to dime size over the Woodbury area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms traveled southeast across southwest Minnesota along with a compact mesoscale convective vortex, with unstable lapse rates leading to brief funnel cloud development.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Despite weaker pre-storm deep-layer shear on the order of 25-30 knots and fairly narrow instability profiles of 1000-1500 J/kg, modifications on the storm scale and an increase in the low-level jet improved the environment enough to support several long-lived supercell clusters along a weak frontal boundary.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unstable warm sector with MUCAPE up to 2000-2500 J/kg developed during the afternoon between an advancing cold front across southeast South Dakota, and a gangly warm front extending into southwest Minnesota. An approaching weak impulse spurred rapid development of multiple rounds of storms near the warm front, initially discrete, with upscale growth into short bowing segments. Secondary development occurred during the evening along the cold front, which evolved into a slow-moving linear system with hail, wind, and heavy rainfall with shear vectors increasingly parallel to the boundary.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A narrow path of crop damage, possibly related to a miso vortex within a broader wind field, was identified using Sentinel satellite imagery.

There were several surges along the broken line of storms that produced severe wind gusts and damaging winds. A few large hailstones also occurred within this line of storms. As the storms moved eastward, the severity lowered with wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A secondary wave of severe thunderstorms developed over southwest Minnesota by the late afternoon of Saturday, August 28th. This area of storms formed a bow-echo that moved from Springfield, east-northeast toward New Ulm, Mankato, and Le Center. The most intense part of the bow-echo occurred at the onset that moved across Brown and Watonwan counties. Several tornadoes were documented. Thousands of acres of corn was flattened due to sustained intense winds. As the storm progressed northeast, the bow-echo segment became disorganized south of the Twin Cities metro area. Another wave of storms that originated over southwest Minnesota during the evening, caused strong winds in Brown County but no damage was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Satellite imagery revealed a damage path through crops, and a damage survey noted large branches down in two locations. Time based on radar. QLCS tornado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several storms developed during the afternoon and evening across central and southern Minnesota. Most of the storms were non-severe and producing frequent lightning and gusty winds. One storm that developed southwest of Mankato developed a strong core and dumped up to 2 inch size hail around the Mankato area Sunday evening.

Scattered thunderstorms developed along an increasing thermal gradient across western Minnesota during the afternoon of Friday, August 20th. These storms congealed into small bow segments that produced severe wind gusts near Alexandria, and two brief tornadoes. This area of storms continued to progress eastward during the evening and produced a few wind gusts of 30 to 40 mph with the stronger cells.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several storm chasers documented a tornado that started in Belgrade. It knocked over several trees and numerous large branches in town, and took a few shingles and some soffit off a house. It then moved north-northeast out of town, across open fields, and dissipated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Temperatures averaged 5 to 6 degrees above normal, and precipitation varied between 25 and 50 percent of normal during June. This led to degradation in drought status over much of southwest Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Due to lack of precipitation (0.38 inches at Windom through the first three weeks of the month) and hot temperatures (5 degrees above normal), severe (D2) drought developed late in June. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense storm system moved across the Upper Midwest on December 15th and produced a rare severe weather outbreak across southeast Minnesota. One person was killed near Rochester (Olmsted County) when a large tree fell on the victim. Twelve tornadoes occurred across parts of Mower, Fillmore, Houston, Dodge, Winona and Wabasha Counties. Eight of these tornadoes were rated EF1 with the others rated EF0.  The tornadoes largely moved through rural areas damaging trees, power lines and farm buildings with the exception of an EF0 tornado that moved through the eastern sections of Preston (Fillmore County) and and EF1 tornado that tracked across Homer (Winona County). In addition to the tornadoes, damaging winds occurred with several reports of wind speeds over 70 mph. The highest recorded wind gusts were 85 mph in Plainview (Wabasha County), 84 mph in Harmony (Fillmore County) and Mabel (Fillmore County) and 83 mph south of Grand Meadow (Mower County). A state of emergency was declared in Fillmore County. The Governor of Minnesota authorized emergency assistance for the following counties: Dodge, Fillmore, Houston, Mower, Olmsted and Wabasha.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A barn was destroyed near Le Roy.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A path of wind damage was found near Bristol. An open air farm outbuilding was destroyed, the roof ripped off a large barn, trees and wooded electrical poles were snapped.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following behind the rare December severe weather outbreak, very strong winds buffeted southeast Minnesota from the late evening of December 15th into the morning of the 16th. Wind gusts of 50 to 60 mph were common with a few gusts over 70 mph. The automated weather observing equipment at the Rochester (Olmsted County) airport recorded a wind gust of 77 mph, which was the second highest wind gust on record for this site. In Rochester, the wind caused a fire from a burning transformer to spread to a garage. The high winds caused numerous power outages to occur across the area. The power outages in and around Stewartville (Olmsted County) were so extensive that some outages lasted into December 18th. The Stewartville school district canceled all classes on the 17th because of the lack of power. An elementary school in Rochester also closed on the 17th because of a power outage. A state of emergency was declared in Fillmore County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts of 50 to 60 mph were common across Mower County during the late evening of December 15th into the early morning of the 16th. The automated weather observing equipment at the Austin airport and a mesonet station near Dexter both measured a 58 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms that developed across South Dakota early Saturday morning, August 28th, quickly moved into west central Minnesota and produced up to 3 diameter hail in Swift County. These storms congealed into a fast moving bow-echo that surged across parts of central Minnesota, between Willmar, St. Cloud and Mora. Several areas received very large hail, severe wind gusts and straight-line winds, and there was one tornado south of Albany. As the storms moved over east-central Minnesota, the severity of the storms lessened and only produced isolated wind damage around Milaca. The worst damaged areas were near Glenwood, Sauk Centre, and north and east of St. Cloud where numerous trees were blown down.

An isolated supercell developed across south central South Dakota late evening along an advancing cold frontal boundary. With appreciable DCAPE and deep-layer effective shear, upscale growth occurred rapidly with the development of widespread damaging winds, which spread out of eastern South Dakota into Southwest Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds uprooted and snapped several large trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Temperatures were slightly above normal during the month. The spatial and temporal distribution of rainfall, with more widespread heavier amounts toward the end of the month, defined drought trends. Overall, conditions improved by month's end.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought continued through the first half of August, before worsening late month to extreme (D3) conditions after an 11-day dry period. Conditions reverted to severe drought at the end of the month after 3 to 5 inches of rainfall from August 21 to August 29. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Overall, precipitation near to a little above normal across southwest Minnesota led to the alleviation of areas of severe drought.

Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Temperatures were slightly above normal during the month. The spatial and temporal distribution of rainfall, with more widespread heavier amounts toward the end of the month, defined drought trends. Overall, conditions improved by month's end.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme (D3) drought developed in early August, but conditions returned to severe (D2) with 3 to 5 inches of rainfall between August 21 and August 29. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unstable warm sector with MUCAPE up to 2000-2500 J/kg developed during the afternoon between an advancing cold front across southeast South Dakota, and a gangly warm front extending into southwest Minnesota. An approaching weak impulse spurred rapid development of multiple rounds of storms near the warm front, initially discrete, with upscale growth into short bowing segments. Secondary development occurred during the evening along the cold front, which evolved into a slow-moving linear system with hail, wind, and heavy rainfall with shear vectors increasingly parallel to the boundary.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A narrow path of crop damage, possibly related to a miso vortex within a broader wind field, was identified using Sentinel satellite imagery. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense storm system moved across the Upper Midwest on December 15th and produced a rare severe weather outbreak across southeast Minnesota. One person was killed near Rochester (Olmsted County) when a large tree fell on the victim. Twelve tornadoes occurred across parts of Mower, Fillmore, Houston, Dodge, Winona and Wabasha Counties. Eight of these tornadoes were rated EF1 with the others rated EF0.  The tornadoes largely moved through rural areas damaging trees, power lines and farm buildings with the exception of an EF0 tornado that moved through the eastern sections of Preston (Fillmore County) and and EF1 tornado that tracked across Homer (Winona County). In addition to the tornadoes, damaging winds occurred with several reports of wind speeds over 70 mph. The highest recorded wind gusts were 85 mph in Plainview (Wabasha County), 84 mph in Harmony (Fillmore County) and Mabel (Fillmore County) and 83 mph south of Grand Meadow (Mower County). A state of emergency was declared in Fillmore County. The Governor of Minnesota authorized emergency assistance for the following counties: Dodge, Fillmore, Houston, Mower, Olmsted and Wabasha.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A metal pole shed was destroyed, trees were uprooted and large branches blown down in Racine.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: One person was killed in a chain reaction accident that involved six vehicles on U.S. Highway 52 north of Oronoco (Olmsted County). The area was covered in dense fog that reduced visibility to a half mile or less during the morning of December 15th. Four other people sustained injuries in the accident.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dense fog formed over Olmsted County during the morning of December 15th and reduced the visibility to less than a half mile. A six vehicle chain reaction accident occurred in the fog on U.S. Highway 52 north of Oronoco. One person was killed and four others sustained injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms that developed across South Dakota early Saturday morning, August 28th, quickly moved into west central Minnesota and produced up to 3 diameter hail in Swift County. These storms congealed into a fast moving bow-echo that surged across parts of central Minnesota, between Willmar, St. Cloud and Mora. Several areas received very large hail, severe wind gusts and straight-line winds, and there was one tornado south of Albany. As the storms moved over east-central Minnesota, the severity of the storms lessened and only produced isolated wind damage around Milaca. The worst damaged areas were near Glenwood, Sauk Centre, and north and east of St. Cloud where numerous trees were blown down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large trees and power lines were blown down near Upsala.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Temperatures averaged fairly close to normal during July, but precipitation from 50 to 75 percent of normal allowed for the expansion and degradation of drought conditions across southwest Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought conditions developed during early July.  Temperatures at Lake Wilson ended up 1.2 degrees above normal for the month, with overall near normal precipitation. However, a 14 day stretch from mid- to late-month had less than two-tenths of an inch rainfall. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An elevated supercell storm developed along a mid-level frontal boundary as a weak wave helped to increase and focus the low-level jet. With fairly weak low-level stability, severe winds easily reached the surface along the track of the storm as it transitioned to a linear structure.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gust was measured at automated weather station KMML. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Despite weaker pre-storm deep-layer shear on the order of 25-30 knots and fairly narrow instability profiles of 1000-1500 J/kg, modifications on the storm scale and an increase in the low-level jet improved the environment enough to support several long-lived supercell clusters along a weak frontal boundary.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Corn flattened in a field around one and a half miles south of Holland. Several medium-sized branches also down in tree line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated supercell developed across south central South Dakota late evening along an advancing cold frontal boundary. With appreciable DCAPE and deep-layer effective shear, upscale growth occurred rapidly with the development of widespread damaging winds, which spread out of eastern South Dakota into Southwest Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several 3 to 4 inch tree branches were downed by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A secondary wave of severe thunderstorms developed over southwest Minnesota by the late afternoon of Saturday, August 28th. This area of storms formed a bow-echo that moved from Springfield, east-northeast toward New Ulm, Mankato, and Le Center. The most intense part of the bow-echo occurred at the onset that moved across Brown and Watonwan counties. Several tornadoes were documented. Thousands of acres of corn was flattened due to sustained intense winds. As the storm progressed northeast, the bow-echo segment became disorganized south of the Twin Cities metro area. Another wave of storms that originated over southwest Minnesota during the evening, caused strong winds in Brown County but no damage was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A damage survey noted several trees were damaged at a farmstead, with large branches down. Satellite imagery showed more details to the tornado path. A time-stamped photo from a storm chaser showed a visible funnel in the area where the tornado damage occurred, and the photo was consistent with the tornado being in the flanking line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Temperatures averaged 5 to 6 degrees above normal, and precipitation varied between 25 and 50 percent of normal during June. This led to degradation in drought status over much of southwest Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Due to lack of precipitation (none through the first two weeks of the month 2 miles northeast of Lakefield) and hot temperatures (9 days of 10 or more degrees above normal in the first two weeks), severe (D2) drought developed early in June. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following behind the rare December severe weather outbreak, very strong winds buffeted southeast Minnesota from the late evening of December 15th into the morning of the 16th. Wind gusts of 50 to 60 mph were common with a few gusts over 70 mph. The automated weather observing equipment at the Rochester (Olmsted County) airport recorded a wind gust of 77 mph, which was the second highest wind gust on record for this site. In Rochester, the wind caused a fire from a burning transformer to spread to a garage. The high winds caused numerous power outages to occur across the area. The power outages in and around Stewartville (Olmsted County) were so extensive that some outages lasted into December 18th. The Stewartville school district canceled all classes on the 17th because of the lack of power. An elementary school in Rochester also closed on the 17th because of a power outage. A state of emergency was declared in Fillmore County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts of 50 to 60 mph were common across Houston County during the late evening of December 15th into the early morning of the 16th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado developed 4 miles west-northwest of Iona, approximately one-third of a mile west of 105th Avenue along 61st Street in an open field. The tornado tracked northeast for a little over a mile before it dissipated along 71st Street one-quarter mile east of 105th Avenue near the entrance to the Murray County landfill.  From Sentinel satellite imagery and a ground survey, the average path width was determined to be 30 yards as it passed through corn fields.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Identified from Sentinel satellite imagery and confirmed by ground survey, a tornado spun up one-half mile southwest of Dovray just east of 250th Avenue.  The tornado moved north over marsh and farmland, and caused damage to crops and cattails. The tornado dissipated one-half mile west of Dovray, just north of State Highway 30. The average path width was 20 yards.

One person was killed near Rochester (Olmsted County) when a large tree fell on the victim. Twelve tornadoes occurred across parts of Mower, Fillmore, Houston, Dodge, Winona and Wabasha Counties. Eight of these tornadoes were rated EF1 with the others rated EF0.  The tornadoes largely moved through rural areas damaging trees, power lines and farm buildings with the exception of an EF0 tornado that moved through the eastern sections of Preston (Fillmore County) and and EF1 tornado that tracked across Homer (Winona County). In addition to the tornadoes, damaging winds occurred with several reports of wind speeds over 70 mph. The highest recorded wind gusts were 85 mph in Plainview (Wabasha County), 84 mph in Harmony (Fillmore County) and Mabel (Fillmore County) and 83 mph south of Grand Meadow (Mower County). A state of emergency was declared in Fillmore County. The Governor of Minnesota authorized emergency assistance for the following counties: Dodge, Fillmore, Houston, Mower, Olmsted and Wabasha.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A path of wind damage occurred south of Preston. Trees were blown down and roofs were removed from a couple of cattle barns.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A secondary wave of severe thunderstorms developed over southwest Minnesota by the late afternoon of Saturday, August 28th. This area of storms formed a bow-echo that moved from Springfield, east-northeast toward New Ulm, Mankato, and Le Center. The most intense part of the bow-echo occurred at the onset that moved across Brown and Watonwan counties. Several tornadoes were documented. Thousands of acres of corn was flattened due to sustained intense winds. As the storm progressed northeast, the bow-echo segment became disorganized south of the Twin Cities metro area. Another wave of storms that originated over southwest Minnesota during the evening, caused strong winds in Brown County but no damage was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A narrow path of crop damage, possibly related to a misovortex within a broader wind field, was identified using Sentinel satellite imagery. It was not possible to correlate the crop damage with any radar circulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Temperatures were slightly above normal during the month. The spatial and temporal distribution of rainfall, with more widespread heavier amounts toward the end of the month, defined drought trends. Overall, conditions improved by month's end.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme (D3) drought continued in early August, but conditions returned to severe (D2) with 1 to 2 inches of rainfall between August 21 and August 29. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.
